metadata.full_json:
{"id": "img_69", "source_md": "Does energy transition improve air quality_ Eviden.md", "save_path": "C:\liuxingyu\multisubspace-data\predataprocess\paper_md\Does energy transition improve air quality_ Eviden\figure_2.jpg", "title": "Fig. 2. Heating area and coal consumption (1997-2018).", "references": ["Due to the lower temperatures in Northern China, burning coal for heating had become the most common way in which residents kept warm in winter. Since the 1950s, the Government of China provided winter heating via coal-fired boilers in the north Huai River areas. Northern cities received free unlimited heating from November 15 to March 15. Up until now, heating has not only been an important indicator of social wealth but has also become necessary for the survival of northern people in winter. According to statistical data released by the Ministry of Housing and Urban-Rural Development (MOHURD) of the People's Republic of China, the annual growth rate of the urban centralized heating area reached  $14.04\%$  during 1991-2017 (Fig. 2). At the same time, coal consumption increased under the rising demand for heating in the two decades prior to 2013. The "coal-to-gas/electricity" policy has generally altered the previous energy consumption structure. However, the rural heating energy consumption structure has largely remained unbalanced, among which coal consumption accounts for approximately  $75\%$ . Moreover, the large scattered coal heating demand has caused a series of environmental and social problems. The combustion of coal in boilers is associated with the release of air pollutants, especially  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  and total suspended particles (TSP). Winter heating has also reduced people's life expectancy in northern regions of China [27]. To meet the increasing demand for heating as well as to alleviate air pollutant emissions, the National Development and Reform Commission of the People's Republic of China announced its plan for the WCHP project (covering 2017-2021).", "implementing intervention measures have seen substantial air quality improvements [7,8]. For example, the United States of America has replaced coal with natural gas in generating electric power since 1997 and has achieved  $44\%$  and  $40\%$  reduction in  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  and  $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$  emissions from the 1997 levels [9]. A cleaner heating technologies program in New Zealand has led to the significant decline of PM emissions and concentrations [10]. Such policies aiming to diversify energy structures with clean and renewable energy have been successful in certain countries, which also has important implications for China [11]. In Northern China, the annual consumption of coal used for heating is approximately 400 million metric tons, nearly  $50\%$  of which is separately distributed for use in rural areas. Moreover, the combustion of scattered coal heating has caused severe haze to occur in winter [12]. Chen et al. found that the combustion of scattered coal heating has led to an approximately two-thirds increase in air pollutants during periods when heating is employed (i.e., winter) compared to periods when heating is not employed (i.e., summer) [13]. Compared to coal, clean energies such as natural gas and wind power emit less  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$ ,  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , and  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ , and thus reduce human health risks from exposure to air pollutants [9]. Therefore, switching from scattered coal", "Studies on the effectiveness of energy transition have not produced a consensus among scholars. Lin and Zhu [18] found that fuel switching in "energy saving and emission reduction" (ESER) policy had failed to decrease  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations. Li and Chen [19] reported that a city that underwent coal-to-gas transition had no significant impact on reducing  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  emissions, and this was primarily because the city focused on the replacement of industrial coal while ignoring the combustion of scattered coal heating in rural areas. Additionally, although Zhao and Luo [20] reported that a "coal-to-gas" project decreased pollutant emissions from a household sector by substituting civil urban coal consumption, they confirmed that this project had a limited effect on reducing industrial coal consumption. Liu et al. [21] adopted the GAINS model to examine the effect of coal switching strategy in Jing-Jin-Ji region, and estimated that the primary  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  decreased by  $28\%$  and  $11\%$ , respectively. Wu et al. [22] evaluated the household coal-replacement program in North China and concluded that coal-to-electricity policy achieved the goal of a clean winter, while the high-quality coal replacement policy had no improvement in indoor air quality. Since the  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  (smog) crisis in the winter of 2013, environmental governance in China has shifted attention to scattered coal heating in rural areas, after which a series of policies were implemented and subsequently achieved remarkable results [23]. However, official reports may have overestimated the benefits of the clean heating policy by simply attributing the decrease in air pollutant concentrations to the promotion of clean heating policies while ignoring the interference of other exogenous energy-saving and emission-reduction policies [24]. Additionally, policy impacts will vary in different regions; thus, it is necessary to identify the validity of such policy among regions. Few studies to date have focused on the evaluation of energy transition policies in China. As an important part of China's energy transition process, it must be determined that whether or not the "Winter Clean Heating Pilot" (WCHP) project improves air quality. Answering this question can offer some guidance in China's future environmental governance while also providing a quantitative reference for other coal-based, energy-consuming developing countries. It could also be beneficial to achieve the synergy between emission reduction and air quality improvement under the background of climate change.", "On the one hand, even though the WCHP project has proven to be an effective mean to control winter-derived heating emissions, the cost of this project should not be ignored. The conversion cost of "coal-to-gas/electricity" is higher and includes a subsequent increase in the cost of gas. Replacing unprocessed coal with clean coal can be used as a transitional method in the ultimate goal of energy transition for regions that experience considerable financial governmental pressure and low residential income. The government should focus on reforming the cost of electricity/gas by updating to more advanced heating technology and devices. On the other hand, since the reduction effect of the WCHP project on regional air quality varies under different development levels and resource distributions, project control should be delegated to regional governments based on local features rather than following the outdated pattern of "one-size-fits-all". In order to minimize the negative impact of clean heating policies on energy poverty and energy consumption inequality, the government should provide subsidies to clean energy more affordable to low-income groups.", "[1] Pope lii CA, Burnett RT, Thun MJ, Calle EE, Krewski D, Ito K, et al. content.title: Fig. 2. Comparisons of emission uncertainties of coal-fired power plants in Guizhou in 2005 using sector-based and unit-based methods. The “+” represents the central value and the box represents the  95\%  CI using the unit-based method, while the larger range line represents the  95\%  CI using the sector-based method.
content.summary: Figure 2 compares emissions uncertainties of coal-fired power plants in Guizhou, 2005, using sector-based and unit-based methods. Central values and 95% CIs are shown for each pollutant.
content.insight: The figure demonstrates that using a unit-based method reduces emission uncertainty compared to the sector-based approach, highlighting the importance of detailed source information.

content.title: Fig.4 Number of industrial heat source objects and industrial heat anomaly points in Beijing-Tianjin-Hebei and its surrounding areas

content.insight: The figure suggests a decline in industrial heat sources and anomalies, reflecting effective policy implementation and industrial transformation efforts in the Beijing-Tianjin-Hebei region.

{"id": "img_223", "source_md": "一种工业热源提取方法_利用温度特征模板的BP神经网络.md", "save_path": "C:\liuxingyu\multisubspace-data\predataprocess\paper_md\一种工业热源提取方法_利用温度特征模板的BP神经网络\figure_4.jpg", "title": "Fig.4 Number of industrial heat source objects and industrial heat anomaly points in Beijing-Tianjin-Hebei and its surrounding areas", "supplement": "(a) 工业热源对象 (b) 工业热源热异常点 图4 京津冀及周边地区工业热源对象和工业热异常点数量 ", "references": ["Abstract: Aiming at the problem of insufficient quantity and spatial refinement in the extraction of industrial heat source from annual scale thermal anomaly data, a neural network industrial heat source extraction method based on temperature feature template is proposed by using VIIRS active fire data. This study took Beijing-Tianjin- Hebei and its surrounding areas as the study area, Firstly, according to the spatial aggregation characteristics of industrial heat sources, the heat source objects were divided by the OPTICS algorithm. Secondly, according to the thermal radiation characteristics of the heat sources, the temperature characteristic template of industrial heat sources and non- industrial heat sources were constructed. Finally, the BP neural network was used to extract industrial heat source objects using the temperature feature template and heat source statistical characteristics as parameters. The results show that: (1) the extraction precision of industrial heat source of the neural network algorithm of temperature feature template proposed in this paper reached  $96.31\%$ . Compared with time filtering and logistic regression methods, the extraction precision of industrial heat sources was improved by  $8.45\%$  and  $7.53\%$ , respectively; (2) From 2015 to 2020, the number of industrial heat sources in the six provinces and cities in Beijing-Tianjin- Hebei and its surrounding areas decreased by  $27.46\%$ . The number of industrial heat source objects and heat anomalies in Hebei Province decreased by  $8.06\%$  and  $7.44\%$  annually, respectively, which was the largest decrease compared with other provinces and cities. The", "concentration of industrial heat sources in Shandong and Tianjin increased by  $25.72\%$  and  $86.64\%$ , respectively, indicating that the industrial transformation and upgrade policies in the two places have achieved remarkable results; (3) Tangshan, Handan, Lvliang, and Changzhi accounted for  $31.37\%$  of the total industrial heat sources in the study area, which are the main cities in Beijing-Tianjin-Hebei and its surrounding areas. The degree of industrial heat source accumulation and energy consumption in seven cities such as Linfen and Taiyuan was higher than those in other cities; The degree of industrial heat source accumulation and energy consumption in 11 cities such as Beijing and Zhoukou was lower than those in other cities; (4) From January to May 2020, the number of industrial heat anomalies in Beijing-Tianjin-Hebei and its surrounding areas remained unchanged or increased compared with the same period in 2019 and 2021. The COVID-19 had no significant impact on the industrial heat source in the study area. The number of industrial heat anomalies in Wuhan in January and February 2020 decreased by more than  $66.67\%$  compared with that in the same period in 2019 and 2021, the number of industrial heat anomalies from March to May 2020 was lower than that in the same period of 2019. The COVID-19 has had a significant impact on industrial heat sources in Wuhan from January to May 2020. This study reflects the current situation and trend of industrial heat source development in Beijing-Tianjin-Hebei and its surrounding areas, which provides a valuable reference for the formulation and adjustment of relevant policies such as reducing energy consumption and improving secondary industry concentration.", "Key words: industrial heat source; OPTICS algorithm; temperature feature template; neural network; VIIRS Active Fire; Beijing-Tianjin-Hebei and its surrounding areas", "(d) Slope_NFHWH

content.title: Fig. 3. The analysis framework of the sources for energy increase in the passenger transport sector in China.
content.summary: The diagram outlines the sources of energy increase in China's passenger transport sector, detailing sub-sectors and influential factors like population and vehicle stock structure.
content.insight: The framework highlights the impact of policy measures on energy efficiency, emphasizing transportation structure adjustment and fuel economy improvements for significant energy savings.

metadata.full_json:
{"id": "img_74", "source_md": "Energy growth sources and future energy-saving pot.md", "save_path": "C:\liuxingyu\multisubspace-data\predataprocess\paper_md\Energy growth sources and future energy-saving pot\figure_3.jpg", "title": "Fig. 3. The analysis framework of the sources for energy increase in the passenger transport sector in China.", "references": ["analysis framework is proposed to consider different characteristics of traffic modes, as shown in Fig. 3. The effects of main driving factors on total passenger transport energy use are analyzed briefly. Furthermore, a specific investigation into three subsectors of passenger transport is performed in turn.", "# Energy growth sources and future energy-saving potentials in passenger transportation sector in China", "In China, energy use from passenger vehicles is increasing rapidly due to urbanization and income growth, but the contributions of transport service structure shift and energy efficiency improvement, especially future energy-saving directions are controversial and unclear because of the differences in the choices of traffic modes, which may greatly affect the analysis results and their interpretations. This study investigates the growth sources of energy use in the passenger transport sector during 1979-2017 and future energy-saving potentials by 2030 by re-examining statistical data. Results show that the transport service increase and transport structure shift led to the increase in energy use by  $665.25\%$  and  $337.22\%$ , but approximately  $14\%$  of the increased energy use was offset by energy efficiency improvements, and its effect is limited in public transportation sector, compared with private passenger vehicle sector. In public passenger transport sector, adjusting the service or volume structure may be a desirable option while in private passenger vehicle sector the policy focus is more on improving fuel efficiency and reducing average annual kilometers traveled per capita. Overall, there is still at least  $28.3\%$  of energy reduction potential by energy efficiency improvement before 2030 compared to 2017 baseline level. Potential evaluation showed that the current potential of transport service structure adjustment based on industrial planning is limited, which highlights the necessity of reform, involving promoting the sharing transportation, railway, subway and buses.", "In the last two decades, the transport sector has become a major source of energy consumption worldwide. In China, transport energy use increased from 58.63 Mtce (million tons of coal equivalent) in 1995 to 421.91 Mtce in 2017, with an annual average growth rate of nearly  $10\%$ . Energy use from passenger transport dominates total transport energy use, accounting for nearly  $60\%$  in 2017. By 2030, more than 300 million new rural residents are expected to enter the cities in China, which will have a great impact on the passenger transport energy consumption. Passengers are relatively easily guided by policy measures to change their energy-using behaviors in their daily travel, thus leading to low-carbon", "However, one of the key challenges is that the current energy statistics of passenger transportation are fragmented in China. The lack of comprehensive statistic specification causes that the data is scattered into different sectors in energy statistical system. The data does not properly cover metrics of interest for energy consumption analysis of passenger transportation. Part of the data is covered in the Transport, Storage and Post sector and Residential Consumption sector in China Energy Statistical Yearbook [3]. The former only includes the intercity public passenger transport and", "Many studies have investigated energy consumption and emissions increase in transport sector [2,5,6]. The relationship between transport energy use and socio-economic factors has also been intensively discussed using econometric models [7-11]. Various options to save energy and reduce emissions in the transport sector have been evaluated for their reduction potential, from both the technological and behavioral perspectives [1,12,13]. In transport studies, bottom-up methodology and scenario analysis were often used to study future trends, mitigation options, and reduction potential [14,15]. In particular, road transport and passenger transport have always been the hot topics for evaluating energy consumption and emissions [16,17].", "Either from an economy-wide or a sectoral perspective, the driving forces of increased energy consumption have been extensively investigated [18-20]. In the transportation sector, these studies could be summarized from two perspectives, namely, 1) the sectoral perspective, and 2) the vehicle-based perspective. From the sectoral perspective, changes in energy consumption and emission have been analyzed either for the whole transportation sector or its subsectors. Activity factors, namely Gross Domestic Product (GDP) growth, population increase, and propensity to travel, were often found to be the main drivers of growing energy consumption [21-24], which is similar to the passenger transport subsector [25-27]. Other studies have focused more on micro sections of transportation. For example, on the vehicle level, the vehicle population, fuel mix, and vehicle fuel efficiency were", "With accelerating urbanization in China, passenger transport energy use has rapidly increased, from 28.78 Mtce in 1997 to 252.47 Mtce in 2017, with an annual average growth rate of  $11.47\%$  (Fig. 1). Energy use could thus be divided into three development stages. From 1978 to 1990, the intercity public passenger transport was the main traveling mode of passengers, as the urban and the private vehicle subsectors were undeveloped. From 1990 to 2002, energy consumption of the private passenger vehicles gradually caught up with that of the intercity subsector. From 2003 to 2017, the private subsector became the major source of the passenger transport energy use. The energy intensity of passenger transport decreased continually due to technological advances, and the energy intensity of the private passenger vehicles decreased rapidly from 143.68 Tce/Mp-km (ton coal equivalent/million passenger-kilometers) in 1997 to 51.38 Tce/Mp-km in 2017, due to the policy of fuel consumption limits for passenger cars [48]. This corresponds to a huge reduction in fuel consumption. Without this reduction in energy intensity, the fuel consumption in the private passenger transport sector would have increased nearly three times greater than it is today.", "(c) Modal energy intensity  
Fig. 7. Energy consumption and passenger volume share of three modes of the urban public passenger transport, 1978-2017. Data sources: China Statistical Yearbook [4]; China Urban Construction Statistical Yearbook [69]; Statistics bulletin of transportation sector development [64]; Fuel consumption limits for passenger cars, Fuel consumption evaluation methods and targets for passenger cars [48] and the authors' personal calculation.", "The data includes socio-economic activity, transport structure and energy intensity. The socio-economic activity data includes the population, the number of households and the GDP, which are from the China Statistical Yearbook [4]; the transport-related activity data includes the passenger service of the intercity public passenger transport, the passenger volume of the urban public passenger transport and the vehicle population of the private passenger vehicles, which are from 1) Passenger Kilometers of the Transport, Postal and Telecommunication Services, and Software Industry, 2) Basic Statistics on Public Transportation in Cities by Region of the Urban, Rural and Regional Development, 3) Possession of Private Vehicles of the Transport, Postal and Telecommunication Services, and Software Industry in the China Statistical Yearbook [4]. The structure data consists of the service structure of the intercity public modes, the volume structure of the urban public modes and the vehicle type structure of the private subsector, which are calculated based on the above transport-related activity data. The intensity data includes the energy intensity of the three subsectors and the average annual mileage of the private passenger vehicles, which are from the Yearbook of China Integrated transport [63], the Statistics bulletin of transportation sector development [64], the Railway Statistics [65], and the Fuel consumption limits for passenger cars [48]. The average annual mileage of the private passenger vehicles is from the survey data in the FEEI model [49].", "The GDP per capita effect was the dominant contributor to energy consumption growth and its contribution increased over time, but the contribution decreased during 1996-2000 (Figs. 5b) and 2008-2010 (Fig. 5c) due to the Asian financial crisis and the global financial crisis respectively. The shift of various modes of passenger transport (structure effect) contributed to the energy consumption decrease in the years leading up to 1990 because the passenger service share of civil aviation decreased (Fig. 5c). Since 1990, the structure effect turned from a negative value to a positive value. After 1990, the highway and civil aviation services were promoted vigorously by the State Council and Civil Aviation Administration of China [50,66]. Therefore, adjusting the transport structure in the intercity subsector, such as promoting the transfer form highway and civil aviation to railway, could be an effective way to save energy. The passenger service intensity effect provides an increasingly negative contribution to the increase in energy consumption during 1978-2017. The energy intensity of the railway, waterway, and civil aviation services decreased rapidly due to technological advancements and improved management.", "The GDP per capita effect was the dominant contributor to energy consumption growth due to rapid economic development in China. The most prominent contribution appeared during 1991-1995 and 2006-2010, with an energy consumption increase of  $275\%$  and  $195\%$ , respectively (Fig. 6b). The passenger volume structure effect was another main driver of the energy consumption growth due to the shift in the dominant mode of transport. From the late 1980s to the 1990s, the taxi industry grew rapidly, owing to the policy of open business implemented by the State Council", "The multiplication decomposition results in the three subsectors are shown in Fig. 9. Five influential factors on energy consumption can be observed and are quite similar in their effects in the intercity and urban passenger transport subsectors. The GDP per capita increase was always the dominant contributor to rises in energy consumption, but this effect was larger in the urban subsector (2.713). The passenger volume/service per GDP played a critical role in reducing energy consumption as opposed to energy intensity in the public transportation sector. In contrast, however, the private transport subsector showed that fuel efficiency improvement, decline of family size and average annual kilometers per capita offset the effect of vehicle stock growth per capita together.", "This study contributes to understanding the underlying driving factors of energy growth and exploring energy efficiency improvement opportunities in passenger transport sector in China. First, a comprehensive picture of passenger transport energy consumption trends during 1979-2017 in China is given. Main drivers of energy growth are briefly identified through re-classifying the fragmented data statistics, which, to our knowledge, are unique and original. Second, the effects of the main driving forces of increased energy consumption are investigated firstly by using a hybrid decomposition method. Third, the future energy-saving potentials of different policy pathways until 2020 and 2030 are estimated and compared involving sharing transportation, structure adjustment, energy efficiency improvement, and urban car ownership control.", "The results show that there are diversified contributors to energy consumption depending on the traffic modes utilized. On the whole, GDP growth per capita, motorization level and shifts in service structure mainly contribute to energy consumption increase in passenger transport sector during the period 1979-2017, and they cause more than 10 times increase in energy consumption, but the actual energy consumption increased just by 8.77 times because of the effect of energy efficiency improvement. For the intercity and urban public passenger transport, the GDP growth per capita, the service or volume structure change, and population growth strongly dominate the changes in energy consumption, and the effect of energy efficiency improvement is not very significant on a relative basis. In the private passenger vehicle sector, however, the situation is significantly different. Fuel efficiency improvement, decline of family size and average annual kilometers traveled per capita together offset the positive impacts from motorization level rise and vehicle type structure change. Especially, the fuel efficiency improvement has enormously contributed to energy conservation since 2010 in China.", "Outlook analysis shows that by 2030 energy conservation potential in intercity public passenger transport sector is limited (16.6%), but there are still huge energy-saving potentials in urban public passenger transport sector (54.3%) and private passenger transport sector (34.3%). They could totally lead to 31.6% of energy consumption reduction in passenger transport sector by 2030.", "In order to reduce energy use in passenger transport sector, multiple policy directions deserve attention. In China, the motorization level could be regulated by some mandatory policies, such as the vehicle license lottery in major cities, such as Beijing and Tianjin. In future, it is essential to give priority to public transport and sharing transport, such as the subway, bus, car-sharing, and bike-sharing. Particularly, first, sharing transportation deserves more attention in China because Chinese people are more likely to accept sharing and collective ideas, and there are less regulatory restrictions in China. Second, the energy intensity reduction targets up until 2020 need to be strictly enforced, especially for fuel efficiency standards for private passenger vehicles. Third, low-intensity transport modes, such as railway, waterway, rail transit, and mini vehicles, are strongly recommended to promote the low-carbon transition.", "[1] Waisman HD, Guivarch C, Lecocq F. content.title: Fig. 1. The averaged  PM_{2.5}  concentrations during APPCAP (a) and APWBSD (c), and their change in APPCAP (b), and APWBSD (d).
content.summary: The figure illustrates the average PM2.5 concentrations in China during APPCAP and APWBSD phases, showing regional variations and changes.
content.insight: The maps depict significant reductions in PM2.5 concentrations across various regions, highlighting the effectiveness of policy interventions during the APPCAP and APWBSD phases.

metadata.full_json: {"id": "img_129", "source_md": "How the Air Clean Plan and carbon mitigation measu.md", "save_path": "C:\\liuxingyu\\multisubspace-data\\predataprocess\\paper_md\\How the Air Clean Plan and carbon mitigation measu\\figure_1.jpg", "title": "Fig. 1. The averaged  PM_{2.5}  concentrations during APPCAP (a) and APWBSD (c), and their change in APPCAP (b), and APWBSD (d).", "references": ["After seven-year implementing of Air Clean Plan, the mean  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations clearly decreased  $(13.41\\mu \\mathrm{g}\\mathrm{m}^{-3}$ $33.65\\%)$  in China. The average  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations in the first phase (2014-2017) were 35.97  $\\pm 15.11~\\mu \\mathrm{g~m}^{-3}$ , which decreased to  $28.92\\pm 12.76~\\mu \\mathrm{g~m}^{-3}$  in the latter phase (2018-2020) of the China ACP. In both phases, high concentrations were observed mainly in northwest China, northern China, and a small proportion of central China (i.e., Henan and Hubei provinces) (Fig. 1). The  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations decreased in over  $68.09\\%$  of the area in mainland China during the period of APPCAP (Fig. 1b). A clear reduction was detected in BTH (decreased by  $17.33~\\mu \\mathrm{g~m}^{-3}$ ;  $25.81\\%$ ), followed by YRD (decreased by  $12.79~\\mu \\mathrm{g~m}^{-3}$ ;  $24.07\\%$ ) and parts of central China (decreased by  $14.66~\\mu \\mathrm{g~m}^{-3}$ ;  $22.93\\%$ ) during the APPCAP", "Fig. 3 shows the population exposed to ambient  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  in pollution control priority regions and in the whole nation in specific years at the beginning and end of the two phases of ACP (2014, 2017, and 2020). In 2014, over  $80\\%$  of Chinese people lived in areas with  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  over  $35\\mu \\mathrm{g}$ $\\mathrm{m}^{-3}$  (WHO IT-1). In 2020, the corresponding proportion decreased to  $40\\%$ . BTH had the most severe  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  exposure, even with the most prominent air quality improvement amongst four priority regions. In 2014, almost all populations in BTH lived in areas where  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations exceeded the WHO IT-1 threshold and this population proportion decreased to  $90\\%$  in 2020. The FWP was not considered as a priority region for air pollution control in APPCAP, and pollution status was more serious during APPCAP as a result of crude industrial production and its gully terrain that was not conducive to the diffusion of pollutants. By contrast, the effect of ACP measures was profound in FWP region under APWBSD with a  $10\\%$  reduction in the population exposed to  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5} > 35\\mu \\mathrm{g}\\mathrm{m}^{-3}$  in 2020 compared to 2017. The exposed population in PRD is relatively low compared to other priority regions, with  $24\\%$  meeting the WHO IT-1 in 2014 and  $100\\%$  meeting the WHO IT-1 in 2020, which also achieved the \"Beautiful China\" vision. However, almost all populations ( $\\sim 98\\%$  in the whole nation) still lived under the  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations exceeding the WHO IT-3 in 2020, not to mention the WHO AQG standard.", "During the period of APPCAP, the control policies essentially contributed to the decrease of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations (Fig. 8a). The  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  reduction was sourced primarily from the clean production policy (net decrease contribution of  $10.53\\mu \\mathrm{g}\\mathrm{m}^{-3}$ ), followed by the energy consumption control measure (net decrease contribution of  $7.28\\mu \\mathrm{g}\\mathrm{m}^{-3}$ ) and fuel structure optimization (net decrease contribution of  $3.08\\mu \\mathrm{g}\\mathrm{m}^{-3}$ ). Economic growth was the primary driver for increasing  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  pollution from 2014 to 2017, with a net contribution of  $9.00\\mu \\mathrm{g}\\mathrm{m}^{-3}$ . The co-effect of  $\\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  and  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  in this period also intensified  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  pollution. During the APWBSD, the contribution of clean production, energy consumption control and fuel structure optimization to  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  decrease were  $85\\%$ ,  $37\\%$  and  $21\\%$ , respectively. Compared with the period of ACPCAP, the effectiveness of all measures decreases in APWBSD, especially the clean production measures (from  $-257\\%$  to  $-85\\%$  ) and", "We provide a comprehensive estimation of the  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations and the number of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ -attributable premature deaths, as well as spatiotemporal changes in these, and a quantitative assessment of the effectiveness of ACP methods in mainland China during 2014 to 2020. The two key contributions are: (1) We estimated the spatiotemporal variation in the number of deaths attributable to  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  exposure in mainland China during ACP based on the  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  product with the finest spatial resolution available to date, and elucidated the spatial discrepancies in policy implementation across China. China's recent clean air actions have achieved great benefits for human health and the environment. However, over  $98\\%$  of the population still lived under the risk of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  pollution ( $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations over  $15\\mu \\mathrm{g}\\mathrm{m}^{-3}$ ), with 1.12 million ( $95\\%$  CI: 0.79-1.56) premature deaths caused by  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  pollution in mainland China in 2020, accounting for  $\\sim 27.05\\%$  of global ambient  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ -attributable premature deaths. In addition, the environmental and health benefits of China ACP are not fully synergistic in spatial pattern, with the greatest health benefits detected in the YRD region and the best environment benefits identified in the BTH region. To achieve the co-benefits of environment and health, specific policies need to be adopted under different conditions. (2) We quantified the environment and health co-benefits of ACP measures and carbon mitigation from 2014 to 2020. Clean production was the most effective abatement measure in China ACP, reducing net  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations and net premature deaths by  $18.35\\mu \\mathrm{g}\\mathrm{m}^{-3}$  and 0.45 million, respectively. The co-effects of  $\\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  and  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  contributed to a  $23\\%$  and  $26\\%$  decrease in the number of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and premature deaths and became one of the major drivers for  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and premature deaths reduction in the APWBSD phase. This confirms the clear environment and health co-benefits of carbon mitigation measures. Meanwhile, the effectiveness of process and source control measures reduced during 2018 to 2020. With the exhaustion of end-of-pipe and energy consumption control measures, the future air clean actions should focus more on carbon abatement and energy structure optimization, especially under the background of global climate change mitigation. In addition to mitigation in industrial and energy sectors, it is also critical to explore more cost-effective approaches (e.g., agricultural ammonia mitigation) (Zhou et al., 2021; Gu et al., 2021) for pollution control in other sectors.", "Our analysis of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  attributed deaths in China was compared with several recent studies (Table S3). Great progresses was made in previous studies (Zhang et al., 2019a; Li et al., 2019; Ding et al., 2019; McDuffie et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2017; Zou et al., 2019; Apte et al., 2015), albeit with several limitations in common: (1) previous studies focused mainly on the APPCAP (2013-2017) and lack the updated results of the APWBSD (2018-2020); (2) the chemical transport model was used widely in previous research, which showed advantages for predicting the  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations without ground measurement, but also has limitations in terms of prediction accuracy (Ma et al., 2022); (3) the Integrated Exposure Response (IER) model used for premature death estimation in previous studies lacked sufficient information on deaths at high  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  exposure, and failed to separate exposure from ambient  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and secondhand smoke, which could potentially introduce uncertainties (Burnett et al., 2014). Our research provides several advances compared to previous studies. First, we quantified the co-benefit of the Air Clean Plan and carbon mitigation measures in China for the environment and health from 2014 to 2020. Second, we used state-of-the-art  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  products with the highest accuracy (Wang et al., 2021; 2022) (Fig. S3 and Fig. S4 for detail) and the finest resolution (spatial resolution of  $1\\mathrm{km}$ , Fig. S2) at the national scale to offer a full-coverage estimation of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ -attributable premature deaths. With these innovations, we not only provided a more accurate estimation of the risk due to exposure to  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , but also to analyze the great spatially heterogeneity of policy effectiveness. Third, we adopted the updated MR-BRT spline method, which fits the risk with a more flexible shape for more reliable estimation of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ -attributable premature deaths (Hystad et al., 2020). The MR-BRT includes more exposure scenarios at high  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  levels during fitting compared with other models such as the IER model (Yusuf et al., 2020; Chowdhury et al., 2022). It also excludes active smoking to control for and reduce potential bias due to this effect (Pope et al., 2018). Finally, our study estimated the co-benefit from  $\\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  and  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  to the environment and health under the background of the ACP and NDC pledges for the first time."], "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Results & Findings", "sub_roles": ["Present Quantitative Results", "Showcase Prediction Results"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Geospatial Visualizations", "sub_types": ["Map"]}, "key_entities": ["PM2.5 concentrations", "APPCAP", "APWBSD", "China", "BTH", "YRD"], "concise_summary": "The figure illustrates the average PM2.5 concentrations in China during APPCAP and APWBSD phases, showing regional variations and changes.", "inferred_insight": "The maps depict significant reductions in PM2.5 concentrations across various regions, highlighting the effectiveness of policy interventions during the APPCAP and APWBSD phases."}

content.title: Fig. 1. Trends in GDP, energy consumption, and population, as well as emissions of  SO_2 ,  NO_x , primary  PM_{2.5} , non-methane volatile organic compounds (NMVOCs), and  CO_2  between 1990 and 2017, with values for 1990 taken as 1. GDP, energy consumption, and population data were obtained from the National Bureau of Statistics of China. Emission data of air pollutants ( SO_2 ,  NO_x ,  PM_{2.5} , and NMVOCs) for 2010-2017 was obtained from Tsinghua University's multi-resolution emission inventory for China (MEIC) [5].  CO_2  emission data was obtained from the International Energy Agency (IEA) [6].

content.title: Fig. 4. The architecture of the tailored CNN-LSTM network.
content.summary: The figure illustrates the CNN-LSTM architecture used for air quality prediction, showcasing the model's encoder-decoder structure and workflow for feature extraction and forecasting.
content.insight: The tailored CNN-LSTM model is designed to effectively capture temporal-spatial correlations in air quality data, reducing training issues and improving prediction accuracy.

{"id": "img_25", "source_md": "Air quality changes in China 2013-2020_ Effectiven.md", "save_path": "C:\liuxingyu\multisubspace-data\predataprocess\paper_md\Air quality changes in China 2013-2020_ Effectiven\figure_4.jpg", "title": "Fig. 4. The architecture of the tailored CNN-LSTM network.", "references": ["Considering the data features of high-dimension, multivariable, nonlinearity and temporal-spatial correlation, and with the application of Keras and TensorFlow, a predictive model combining CNN and LSTM is applied and tailored in this study. The architecture and workflows are shown in Fig. 4 and summarized as follows.", "As shown in Fig. 4, the general architecture of the predictive model is an encoder-decoder structure. The first half of the network is CNN and is utilized for feature extraction of input data. The latter half is LSTM, analyzing the features extracted by CNN and then forecasting the AQI of the next point in time.", "backpropagation and learning the input sequence with longer time steps. Further, Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) have a remarkable advantage in extracting data features and reducing dimensions, which could alleviate the problems of slow training and overfitting in fully connected networks (Alzubaidi et al., 2021). Correspondingly, many variants of the CNN-LSTM combination have been constructed for capturing the spatiotemporal dependencies in air quality forecasting tasks (Donahue et al., 2015; Vinyals et al., 2015; Qin et al., 2019), as it would create a great synergy than the individual models on their own. Specifically, first performing feature extraction using CNN, and then inputting the feature values into the LSTM architecture.", "The study obtained the data including a total of 1440 daily air quality and meteorological data (January 22, 2016, to December 31, 2019) from 31 provincial capital cities in the Chinese mainland,  $60\%$  of which are chosen as the training set,  $20\%$  as the validation set and  $20\%$  as the test set. After determining the best basic network architecture for the current prediction tasks, models are set up in units of cities, and the training set is used to train the tailored CNN-LSTM models. The accuracy indicators of the model prediction results of 31 cities, namely RMSE values averaging 22.06, models perform well overall, indicating that the applied CNN-LSTM network has good capabilities for AQI prediction (Jin et al., 2020; Mao et al., 2021).", "Renew. content.title: Fig. 3. reduction effects of the WCHP project on major pollutants in eastern and western region.
content.summary: The figure illustrates the reduction percentages of major pollutants due to the WCHP project, depicted through changes in central heating areas and coal consumption from 1997 to 2017.
content.insight: The figure demonstrates the effectiveness of the WCHP project in reducing air pollutants, highlighting the reduction in coal dependence due to increased central heating coverage, primarily benefiting air quality in both eastern and western regions.

{"id": "img_70", "source_md": "Does energy transition improve air quality_ Eviden.md", "save_path": "C:\liuxingyu\multisubspace-data\predataprocess\paper_md\Does energy transition improve air quality_ Eviden\figure_3.jpg", "title": "Fig. 3. reduction effects of the WCHP project on major pollutants in eastern and western region.", "references": ["In order to intuitively illustrate the effects of the WCHP project on air pollutants, we separately calculated percentage reduction effects of this policy in the eastern and western regions (Fig. 3). In the eastern region, decreasing percentages of AQI, $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , $\mathrm{SO}_2$ , and $\mathrm{NO}_2$ were $15.51\%$ , $14.72\%$ , $71.08\%$ , and $31.22\%$ , respectively. In the western region, corresponding decreasing percentages were $25.02\%$ , $22.25\%$ , $65.43\%$ , and $36.49\%$ , respectively. Decreases of most", "Coal used for heating rural households poses a severe threat to ambient air pollution and human health. In 2017, China implemented the Winter Clean Heating Pilot (WCHP) project, which was designed to convert energy from "coal-to-gas/electricity". However, the impact of the WCHP project and its associated dynamic changes have not yet been well quantified. Based on daily monitored panel data covering 35 pilot cities, we conducted a difference-in-differences (DID) model to identify whether or not the WCHP project is effective in maintaining air quality. Results showed that AQI, $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$ , and $\mathrm{SO}_2$ emissions decreased by $20.4\%$ , $18.59\%$ , $34.1\%$ , and $68.4\%$ , respectively, and, except for $\mathrm{SO}_2$ , these pollutant reductions were greater in Western China than in Eastern China. Furthermore, when certain variables were controlled, such as weather, holidays, GDP, the ratio between secondary industries, income levels, and environmental expenditures, these results were also robust. Parallel trends and placebo tests were additionally used to confirm the reliability of the DID model. The implementation of energy transition could gain the synergy between emission reduction and air quality improvement. Policy recommendations were finally made on rural-scale energy transition, including reforming the cost of electricity/gas through advanced technology and subsidies to account for regional inequality.", "Table 4 provides the pollutant reduction effects under the WCHP project using the DID model. As a weighted-average indicator of primary air pollutants, AQI can objectively represent the degree of air pollution [40]. $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ had the highest percentage increment derived from scattered coal combustion during winter heating periods. $\mathrm{SO}_2$ and $\mathrm{NO_x}$ were the main components of acid rain. Hence, we selected AQI, $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , $\mathrm{SO}_2$ , and $\mathrm{NO_x}$ as explanatory variables. Results showed that AQI significantly increased (42.79) during periods when heating was employed, while the WCHP project decreased AQI by 8.72, offsetting approximately $20.38\%$ of the negative impacts caused by winter heating. Likewise, $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ concentrations decreased by $18.59\%$ due to the WCHP project. This finding indicated that the WCHP project has had a significant reduction effect on air pollution during winter heating periods, particularly for $\mathrm{SO}_2$ , since $\mathrm{SO}_2$ emissions decreased significantly by $68.36\%$ . However, the reduction effect on $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ remained far from the percentage released by the official feedback report (i.e., $33.7\%$ ). These results indicated that the net effect was smaller than the", "

metadata.full_json: {"id": "img_71", "source_md": "Does energy transition improve air quality_ Eviden.md", "save_path": "C:\\liuxingyu\\multisubspace-data\\predataprocess\\paper_md\\Does energy transition improve air quality_ Eviden\\figure_4.jpg", "title": "Fig. 4. Estimated coefficients (date) prior to and following the start time of heating.", "references": ["As Fig. 4 shows, the 15 day estimator prior to and following the start of heating varied around a value of zero (0) devoid of a clear trend, and most coefficients were not statistically significant. This result indicated a similarity between pilot cities and non-pilot cities regarding AQI. Despite an abnormal increase 4-6 days following the start time of heating, such differences decreased to a negative value (as seen 2 days prior to the start time of heating), and this trend maintained for the ensuing two weeks. These results were consistent with the hypothesis that the WCHP project generated differential trends in pollutant concentrations between pilot and non-pilot cities. It is noteworthy that the period prior to the start time of heating was implemented and continued for 3-4 days in some cities prior to the formal start time of heating, which may have resulted in the early decrease. Additionally, we also estimated the Heatperiod coefficient 30 days following the start time of heating, which also indicated that effects of the WCHP project existed following the start time of heating.", "where  $Heatperiod_{i,n-1}$  is the set of dummy variables, which represents whether the  $i$ -th city is in a heating period at  $n-1$ -day.  $L_n$  represents a series of estimated coefficients 15 days prior to the start time of heating, and  $E_n$  represents a series of estimated coefficients 15 days following the start time of heating. With the omitted time category being the start time of heating,  $L_n$  captures the differences in pollutant indicators between pilot and non-pilot cities relative to differences just prior to the launch of the policy. Here we selected AQI as the air pollutant proxy. Under such specifications, if the signs of the estimated  $Heatperiod_{i,n-1}$  coefficients differ, it can be assumed that changes in pollutant concentrations are not significant.", "As a second robustness check, we constructed a counterfactual test to check whether our results could be driven by other unobserved factors. In doing so, we selected a different date during the non-heating period (May, June, July, August, September, and October) in lieu of the real start date of heating, specifying the interaction term between the treatment variable and the pseudoheating start time variable as Pseudo-HCP. The WCHP project can only take effect during the heating period, while the pseudo-HCP coefficient can reflect the impact of the WCHP project during the non-heating period. If estimated coefficients of pseudo-WCHP are significant and have the same sign as of the true WCHP shown in Table 4, we can conclude that air quality improvements in pilot cities could not be mainly attributed to the WCHP project, and the robustness of the DID model would be inadequate. In this case, improvements may be related to other pollution control policies, such as production restrictions and limits to the number of private vehicle allowed on roads.", "According to the regressions shown in Table 5, only when the start time of heating was specified in October, coefficients of pseudo-WCHP were significantly negative at a  $1\\%$  level, while other estimation results were not significant or had a positive sign. Compared to Table 4, these conflicting results failed to reject the validity of the \"WHCD\" policy. That is to say, the effect of the WCHP project was evident. In fact, we specified the heating start time on the 15th of November in consideration of the \"official\" start time of central heating while ignoring differences caused by scattered coal combustion. In October, some rural areas in the pilot and non-pilot cities began to burn scattered coal for heating, which could cause an evident increase in pollution emissions [47,48]."], "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Results & Findings", "sub_roles": ["Present Quantitative Results", "Compare Experimental Performance"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Quantitative Charts", "sub_types": ["Bar Chart"]}, "key_entities": ["AQI", "PM2.5", "SO2", "NO2", "Eastern region", "Western region", "WCHP project"], "concise_summary": "The bar chart presents the percentage distribution of AQI, PM2.5, SO2, and NO2 across all samples, and in the Eastern and Western regions.", "inferred_insight": "The figure illustrates regional differences in pollutants, supporting findings that the WCHP project impacts air quality measures across diverse geographical areas."}

metadata.full_json:
{"id": "img_29", "source_md": "Air quality changes in China 2013-2020_ Effectiven.md", "save_path": "C:\liuxingyu\multisubspace-data\predataprocess\paper_md\Air quality changes in China 2013-2020_ Effectiven\figure_8.jpg", "title": "Fig. 8. Correlation coefficients between air quality change and socioeconomic factors.", "references": ["The Kendall correlation coefficients depicted the association between air quality change and socioeconomic factors, as shown in Fig. 8. Ordinary least squares analysis was also done to assess the variance inflation factors, which ranged from 1.007 to 2.297 and were all less than the threshold of 10, indicating that there was no multiple collinearity issue and no high correlation among socioeconomic variables, which made the subsequent correlation analysis more reliable.", "As depicted in Fig. 8, the socioeconomic factors were seen not to be highly associated with the change rate of AQI. Amongst, an obvious observation was that the increase in environmental governance and a decrease in industrialization were positively related to air quality improvement. Furthermore, the increase in economic development,", "Clean and efficient use of coal in China entered the implementation stage in 2015, and a series of policies were established to promote the sustainable development of clean coal technologies (CCTs). However, the long-term effects of the clean coal technology policies (CCTPs) have not been evaluated, and the outbreak of coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) would bring great uncertainty regarding the effectiveness evaluation. Employing deep learning approaches and spatial autocorrelation analysis, the present work intended to explore the air quality variation before and after the implementation of CCTPs, and investigate the association between air quality and socioeconomic factors to explore the internal mechanism of air quality improvement. Results showed that after implementing CCTPs, the air quality index (AQI) had an average reduction of  $18.82\%$  and the identical drop in air pollution in 2018 implied a 2-year time lag of CCTPs. Additionally, emission reduction and deindustrialization were explored as two promising ways to improve air quality while promoting energy transformation. The findings indicated that the Chinese government ought to pay more attention to long-term plans; industrial restructuring and environmental protection should be insisted upon to realize sustainable energy development. By providing a perspective on air quality improvement after the policies are implemented, this study can lead practitioners and academia to a comprehensive and objective view of clean coal policies.", "Different from the previous policy effectiveness studies, which focused on a specific region or policy with little to national and industrial overview, the main goal of this study is to systematically and comprehensively measure the air quality variation after the implementation of CCTPs at the national level. Based on China's air quality and meteorological data from 2013 to 2019, deep learning approaches were applied to forecast the air quality in 2020, eliminating the uncertainties that COVID-19 brought about. Then, the spatial autocorrelation analysis was utilized to investigate the temporal and spatial distribution characteristics of air pollution. And applying socioeconomic data, the association between AQI change and socioeconomic factors was calculated to explore the most likely mechanism of CCTPs. Our findings showed that there was a significant air quality improvement after the implementation of CCTPs, indicating that clean coal transformation adapts to the requirements of sustainable development in China. And two main channels to reduce air pollution were excavated, namely industrialization and environmental governance. This is essential to reaching an agreement on clean coal utilization, supporting the formulation of energy policies, and combating climate change.", "related policies have reached a consensus. Nevertheless, the contributions mainly focus on regions with high-polluting and energy-intensive industries or target a single kind of clean coal policy. Energy transition is a systematic initiative that requires a long-term and holistic vision to guide nations, industries, companies, and individuals to collaborate from all aspects (Wei et al., 2022). The efficient and effective implementation of CCTPs also emphasizes the interrelatedness of social, technical, institutional and political changes (Markard, 2018), and sets the tone for atmospheric administration in a certain period. Therefore, this paper investigates the effectiveness of CCTPs at the national level with the industrial background of coal-based clean energy, and pays attention to how air quality varied after the implementation of CCTPs. Second, in the existing studies, the mechanism of energy transition policies on environmental pollution control is rarely discussed. By clarifying the association between the AQI and socioeconomic factors, this study tries to provide quantitative results for the internal mechanism of air quality improvement.", "where  $C_{AQI}$  is the change rate of the AQI for each studied city,  $pr_{AQI}$  is the average AQI of each month for the studied cities from 2013 to 2015, and  $po_{AQI}$  is the average AQI of each month from 2016 to 2020. The calculated results vary between  $-100\%$  and  $+100\%$ , where negative and positive values indicate improvement or deterioration of air quality, and 0 represents no change.", "To comprehensively explore the association between air quality change and socioeconomic factors, and reflect the internal mechanism of CCTPs working on air quality improvement, the correlation coefficients were calculated with all socioeconomic factors separately using Equation (13). Ordinary least squares regression was also carried out, and the variance inflation factor of each parameter was computed to verify the existence of multicollinearity.", "To better understand the policy effect on air quality improvement, the change rate of AQI was computed between the pre- and post-policy nodes, as shown in Table 4. All the areas experienced improved air quality, with the change rate of AQI ranging from  $4.28\%$  to  $29.54\%$ . Regionally, the degree of improvement was maximum in central China  $(25.61\%)$ , followed by northeast  $(25.47\%)$ , eastern  $(20.88\%)$ , and northern China  $(18.30\%)$ . The three districts with the least improvement were northwest, southern, and southwest China, with change rates of  $15.27\%$ $14.63\%$  and  $14.44\%$  respectively. On average, the AQI value was reduced by  $18.82\%$  from 2013 to 2020, indicating that China's air quality was greatly improved after the implementation of CCTPs.", "During the period of CCTPs implementation, an obvious air quality improvement was observed in the Chinese mainland, manifested as a decrease of AQI value by  $18.82\%$  from 2013 to 2020. It is concluded that the clean and efficient transformation of coal resources conforms to China's national conditions, and could indeed improve the atmospheric environment, which has been demonstrated in the existing research as well (Li and Patino-Echeverri, 2017; Liu et al., 2021). Specifically, correlation coefficients showed that the promotion of emission reduction and deindustrialization could fundamentally help to reduce airborne contamination, although their persistence relies on continued efforts and is influenced by various factors (Yang and Teng, 2018). Another important observation was a 2-year time lag before the stringent CCTPs starting from 2015 takes any strong positive effects, shown as a dramatical reduction of AQI in 2018. It implies that the influence of time lag should be considered in the decision-making process, as a time lag commonly appears in air pollution regulations and plans (Van der et al., 2017).", "Limitations in both data acquisition and method selection should be acknowledged. First, the air quality variation before and after the implementation of CCTPs was investigated over a time span of 7 years, owing to the AQI data is available since 2013. The future study would be enriched with broader periods to observe a long-term performance. For method selection, the first limitation is that only CNN and LSTM were applied, ensemble-based approaches would be explored and incorporated along with the proposed model for further improvement in the prediction performance of the proposed framework. Further, the five socioeconomic factors were extracted and analyzed based on existing research and empirical knowledge. Future studies should continue to examine the deeper mechanisms of CCTPs using more quantitative and modelling methods.", "Renew. Sustain. content.title: Fig. 1. The frequency of sulfur and ash contents in coal and that of removal efficiencies of  SO_2  and  NO_X  for Jiangsu's power plants and other industrial boilers, based on plant-by-plant investigation. The plant numbers are indicated in the parentheses.
content.summary: Fig. 1 illustrates the frequency distribution of sulfur and ash contents in coal, and removal efficiencies of SO2 and NOX for Jiangsu's power plants and industrial boilers.
content.insight: The figure highlights variability in sulfur and ash content across plants and compares the effectiveness of SO2 and NOX removal systems, underscoring the importance of plant-specific data in emissions analysis.

metadata.full_json: {"id": "img_262", "source_md": "Quantifying the uncertainties of China's emission.md", "save_path": "C:\\liuxingyu\\multisubspace-data\\predataprocess\\paper_md\\Quantifying the uncertainties of China's emission\\figure_1.jpg", "title": "Fig. 1. The frequency of sulfur and ash contents in coal and that of removal efficiencies of  SO_2  and  NO_X  for Jiangsu's power plants and other industrial boilers, based on plant-by-plant investigation. The plant numbers are indicated in the parentheses.", "supplement": "(a) For power plants;(b) For other industrial boilers;(c) For power plants;(d) For other industrial boilers", "references": ["To quantify the uncertainties of regional (provincial and city) inventories, Monte Carlo framework was improved by incorporating plant-specific information and then applied at each plant for point sources. As the energy consumption data of power sector were compiled and crosschecked based on multiple data sources (Zhou et al., 2017; Zhao et al., 2015), we assumed that the uncertainty would be smaller than other sources and we tentatively set the CV at  $10\\%$  for each individual plant. For point sources of other categories and area sources, in contrast, the CVs of activity data were set at  $20\\%$ , the same as the national inventory. For emission factors, we divided the parameters into two types, i.e., the common ones and plant-specific ones. The common parameters indicated those that keep stable or change slightly for certain technology/fuel type, e.g., the sulfur and ash release ratio of bituminous pulverized combustion. The PDFs were determined following Zhao et al. (2011, 2013), with the most recent measurement data incorporated. The plant-specific parameters indicated those that are independent and might vary largely between different plants, e.g., the  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$  and  $\\mathrm{NO}_{\\mathrm{x}}$  removal efficiencies of flue gas desulfurization (FGD) and selective catalytic reduction (SCR) systems, respectively, and the sulfur and ash contents of fossil fuels. Available information was collected and comprehensively compiled to generate the PDFs for those parameters. Details are discussed in the following section.", "Plant-specific parameters were applied mainly in uncertainty analysis of point sources in regional inventories (for area sources and point sources without detailed information, common parameters were applied instead). For sources related to fossil fuel combustion (e.g., power plants, industrial boilers/kilns, and industrial processes that contain coal combustion), the PDFs of sulfur content, ash content, and  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$  and  $\\mathrm{NO_X}$  removal efficiencies of corresponding air pollutant control devices (APCDs) were determined for each plant. Fig. 1a and b shows the frequencies of sulfur and ash contents for Jiangsu's power plants and other industrial boilers. The average sulfur content was lower for coals used for power generation than those for other boilers. Normal distributions were assumed for sulfur and ash contents of coal burned for individual plant. Standard deviations of those distributions were determined for each plant with following steps. First, standard deviation for the whole sector was determined through normal distribution fitting with all the data samples within the sector. The difference between sulfur/ash content of single plant and average of the whole sector was then calculated for each plant. The larger value obtained from above two steps was selected as the standard deviation for single plant.", "Fig. 1c and d shows the frequencies of removal efficiencies of  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$  and  $\\mathrm{NO_X}$  for Jiangsu's power and other industrial plants. For both source categories,  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$  control systems were more widely applied than  $\\mathrm{NO_X}$  control, while the average  $\\mathrm{NO_X}$  removal efficiency based on limited data samples was higher than that of  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$  for other industrial boilers. Triangular distribution was assumed for the  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$  removal efficiency for each plant. Clear difference was found between sectors. In 2012,  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$  control technologies (including wetFGD and other simple technologies) were applied at  $90\\%$  of Jiangsu's coal-fired power units, with the average removal efficiency calculated at  $68\\%$ . For industrial boilers and kilns, in contrast,  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$  control was applied only  $18\\%$  of the plants and the average removal rate was  $42\\%$ . Thus the ranges of triangular distributions differed between source categories. For power sector, the ranges of  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$  removal rates for FGD systems and other technologies (e.g., circulating fluidized bed, CFB) were  $50 - 98\\%$  and  $10 - 85\\%$ , respectively, according to the investigated data. For other industrial boilers and kilns, similarly, the ranges were determined at  $50 - 98\\%$ ,  $30 - 85\\%$ ,  $10 - 60\\%$ , and  $10 - 85\\%$  for wet-FGD, dry-FGD, CFB and other technologies, respectively. Besides coal combustion, FGD systems were also applied for other industrial processes. Relatively high removal efficiencies were found for sintering in iron & steel production, and a triangular distribution ranged  $50 - 98\\%$  was assumed for individual plant. For other processes including production of ammonia,", "Table 4 summarized the 1st and 2nd ranking parameters contributing most to the uncertainties of Jiangsu's and Nanjing's emissions in the provincial and city-level inventory by industrial source category, respectively. There were differences in the key parameter identification for the province in the national and provincial inventory. For  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$  from power sector, as an example, the removal efficiencies of FGD for given individual plants were identified as the most significant parameters to the uncertainty, with very limited contribution to variance. Similarly, the uncertainty of emissions from other industrial combustions was estimated to be mostly affected by the sulfur content and FGD removal rate of one single plant. The results thus indicated the importance of better understanding on emission characteristics for certain individual"], "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Results & Findings", "sub_roles": ["Present Quantitative Results", "Showcase Prediction Results"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Quantitative Charts", "sub_types": ["Bar Chart"]}, "key_entities": ["Jiangsu", "power plants", "industrial boilers", "sulfur content", "ash content", "SO2 removal efficiency", "NOX removal efficiency"], "concise_summary": "Fig. 1 illustrates the frequency distribution of sulfur and ash contents in coal, and removal efficiencies of SO2 and NOX for Jiangsu's power plants and industrial boilers.", "inferred_insight": "The figure highlights variability in sulfur and ash content across plants and compares the effectiveness of SO2 and NOX removal systems, underscoring the importance of plant-specific data in emissions analysis."}

content.title: Figure 1. Methodology framework for fusing multisource data to derive daily emission estimates of coal-fired power plants in China.
content.summary: The figure illustrates the methodology framework for fusing multisource data to estimate daily emissions from coal-fired power plants in China, detailing processes like outlier detection and imputation.
content.insight: The framework effectively combines real-time emission data with annual figures to generate accurate daily emissions, compensating for data gaps and variations across plants.

{"id": "img_61", "source_md": "Daily Emission Patterns of Coal-Fired Power Plants.md", "save_path": "C:\liuxingyu\multisubspace-data\predataprocess\paper_md\Daily Emission Patterns of Coal-Fired Power Plants\figure_1.jpg", "title": "Figure 1. Methodology framework for fusing multisource data to derive daily emission estimates of coal-fired power plants in China.", "references": ["Figure 1 shows the methodology framework in this study, including the input database and processes to fuse multisource data to derive daily emission estimates of coal-fired power plant emissions.", "In this work, we develop a framework that fuses plant-level daily profiles from CEMS with yearly emission estimates from CPED to obtain daily emissions (Figure 1). According to guideline HJ/T 75-2017, the maximum volume fraction of  $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  emissions from the combustion of specific fuels remains stable, so we use daily profiles of flue gas volume that takes the oxygen content into account to describe daily variations in  $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  emissions. Daily profiles of emission rates from CEMS are used for major air pollutants of  $\mathrm{SO}_{2}, \mathrm{NO}_{x},$  and  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ .", "# Daily Emission Patterns of Coal-Fired Power Plants in China Based on Multisource Data Fusion", "ABSTRACT: Daily emission estimates are essential for tracking the dynamic changes in emission sources. In this work, we estimate daily emissions of coal-fired power plants in China during 2017-2020 by combining information from the unit-based China coal-fired Power plant Emissions Database (CPED) and real-time measurements from continuous emission monitoring systems (CEMS). We develop a step-by-step method to screen outliers and impute missing values for data from CEMS. Then, plant-level daily profiles of flue gas volume and emissions obtained from CEMS are coupled with annual emissions from CPED to derive daily emissions. Reasonable agreement is found between emission variations and available statistics (i.e., monthly power generation and daily coal consumption). Daily power emissions are in the range of 6267-12,994, 0.4-1.3, 6.5-12.0, and  $2.5 - 6.8\mathrm{Gg}$  for  $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ ,  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_{x}$ , and  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ , respectively, with high emissions in winter and summer caused", "is used to quantify monthly variations in power emissions in a unit-level power plant emission inventory in China.17 Indicator data such as power load curves are employed to indicate daily or hourly dynamics of power emissions.1,18 Recent studies have also attempted to estimate dynamically updated daily power emissions,19-22 which are largely stimulated by the demand for quantifying the evolution of emissions during fast-evolving events (e.g., COVID-19 pandemic). Liu et al.19,20 used daily coal consumption from six major power generation groups in China to estimate daily updated power emissions. However, the daily coal consumption data only covers 62 power plants in China, and the activity indicator-based method is relatively indirect and lacks independent evaluations against observations. Studies utilizing satellite observations to estimate daily emission changes are faced with the challenges of differentiating power emissions from the total21,23 or deriving daily power emissions on continuous time scales.24,25 For example, Ding et al.23 used the  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  emission reductions derived from", "In this work, taking advantage of a well-validated unit-based power emission database covering China and the high temporal resolution of CEMS data, we attempt to construct daily emission estimates of coal-fired power plants in China based on multisource data fusion. Careful screening of outliers and imputation of missing values are conducted for the flue gas volume and emission data. Then, the plant-level daily profiles (i.e., coefficients for day-to-day variations in a year) from CEMS are combined with the unit-level annual emissions from", "2.1.1. Unit-Level Emission Database of Coal-Fired Power Plants in China. The CPED, $^{17}$  developed by Tsinghua University, is a high-resolution emission inventory that provides year-by-year emissions from coal-fired power plants since 1990 in China. Established based on detailed and unit-level information from the Ministry of Ecology and Environment (MEE, undisclosed data), CPED contains unit locations, operational status, unit capacity, coal consumption, control technologies, and emission factors, which enable accurate spatial allocation and emission estimates for power plants. More details about CPED can be found in the paper by Liu et al. $^{16}$  Previous studies have verified that the magnitude and trends of power emissions in CPED are in good agreement with top-down estimates from satellite measurements. $^{43,50}$  Liu et al. $^{43}$  compared  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  emissions in CPED with those based on satellite data and found rather good agreement with the relative differences of  $4 \pm 18\%$ . CPED used here covers 30 provinces (except Hong Kong, Macao, Taiwan, and Tibet), 3948 power plants, and 8722 in-use electricity-generating units from 2017 to 2020.", "In this study, data from CPED and CEMS are fused to improve the daily estimation of emissions from coal-fired power units in China. The comprehensive, highly resolved emission database could capture the dynamic changes in emissions during typical socioeconomic events and could be applied to providing more accurate inputs for CTMs and guiding future policies.", "In previous studies, monthly activity data (e.g., electricity generation and fuel consumption) $^{4}$  and invariable parameters considering factors such as working times $^{1,57}$  have been widely used to disaggregate annual power emissions into monthly and daily scales, respectively. Figure 5 shows comparisons of the multiscale temporal profiles from CEMS with the widely used temporal profiles. At the monthly scale (Figure 5a), the comparison between national average profiles obtained from CEMS and electricity generation shows good agreement ( $R$  between 0.74 and 0.97). The results imply that the commonly used activity indicators are reliable at monthly scale when direct emission measurements are unavailable. At the weekly scale, previous studies $^{1,57}$  use fixed parameters that do not vary over time to allocate emissions from weekly totals to daily scales, considering the empirical production schedule and a few observations, which tend to fail to reflect fluctuations caused by unexpected events. Distinct weekly patterns of power emissions in previous studies $^{1,57}$  are presented in Figure 5b, which are manifested as strong intensity of production activities on weekdays and weak intensity on weekends. Conversely, the weekly profiles from CEMS exhibit no obvious weekend effects, showing that the widely used profiles might exaggerate the differences between weekdays and weekends. Indeed, in many developed countries, $^{58}$  power demand on working days is usually higher than that on weekends. However, owing to different lifestyle, economic, and cultural background, there is no significant difference in electricity demand and power generation between weekdays and weekends in China. And unlike peaking power plants (e.g., natural gas power plants and hydroelectric facilities), coal-fired", "In this work, we estimate daily emissions from coal-fired power plants in China during 2017-2020 through data fusion of a well-validated unit-based power emission database and high-temporal-resolution measurements from CEMS. A step-by-step method is developed to screen outliers and impute missing values for data from CEMS, and sensitivity tests show that monthly and weekly temporal profiles by different imputation methods are in good agreement ( $R = 0.90 - 0.98$  for national scale,  $R = 0.61 - 0.92$  for provincial level). We find that the temporal profiles from CEMS agree well with available statistical data like monthly power generation and daily coal consumption, with  $R$  greater than 0.7 for 16-27 out of 30 provinces at the monthly scale and  $R$  up to 0.83 at the daily scale. Daily power emissions from the fused database are in the range of 6267-12,994, 0.4-1.3, 6.5-12.0, and  $2.5 - 6.8\mathrm{Gg}$  for  $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ ,  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ , and  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ , respectively, during 2017-2020, reflecting higher emissions in winter and summer caused by heating and cooling demand and lower ones resulting from holidays. Our estimates are also able to capture the sudden changes in daily power emissions during typical socioeconomic events, including the much slower rebound of power emissions after the Spring Festival holiday due to the impact of COVID-19, higher daily  $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  emissions from thermal power generation in Yunnan owing to a severe drought, and significant decline of power emissions in Wuhan caused by a short-term emission control during the 7th CISM Military World Games. We also find that previous studies (e.g.,  $\mathrm{EDGAR}^1$ ) that apply fixed parameters to allocate weekly power emission totals to daily scales exaggerate the differences between weekdays and weekends. This work provides a framework to propagate information from real-time emission measurements to high-temporal-resolution emission estimates, which will support dynamic emission tracking and air pollution modeling.", "(6) Nassar, R.; Napier-Linton, L.; Gurney, K. content.title: Fig. 6. (a) Trends in observed annual  PM_{2.5}  concentrations for BTH and 74 key cities between 2013 and 2017; (b) trends expressed as 90th percentiles in maximum daily average  8hO_3  concentration. Shaded areas represent the 25th-75th percentile range. Data are available in Table S1 in Appendix A. Details on monthly  PM_{2.5}  concentration for 74 key cities are summarized in Tables S2-S6.  PM_{2.5}  and  O_3  concentrations in the BTH region and 74 cities were obtained from the China National Environmental Monitoring Center.
content.summary: The figure shows trends in PM2.5 and O3 concentrations between 2013 and 2017 in BTH and 74 key cities, highlighting a decrease in PM2.5 and an increase in O3 levels.
content.insight: Despite reductions in PM2.5, O3 pollution increased, posing challenges in simultaneous mitigation and highlighting the need for integrated pollution control strategies in China.
