metadata.full_json: {"id": "img_180", "source_md": "Progress of Air Pollution Control in China and Its.md", "save_path": "C:\\liuxingyu\\multisubspace-data\\predataprocess\\paper_md\\Progress of Air Pollution Control in China and Its\\figure_6.jpg", "title": "Fig. 6. (a) Trends in observed annual  PM_{2.5}  concentrations for BTH and 74 key cities between 2013 and 2017; (b) trends expressed as 90th percentiles in maximum daily average  8hO_3  concentration. Shaded areas represent the 25th-75th percentile range. Data are available in Table S1 in Appendix A. Details on monthly  PM_{2.5}  concentration for 74 key cities are summarized in Tables S2-S6.  PM_{2.5}  and  O_3  concentrations in the BTH region and 74 cities were obtained from the China National Environmental Monitoring Center.", "references": ["Despite the substantial progress that has been made in addressing acid rain and  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  issues (Fig. 6(a)), an unwanted increase in ground-level  $\\mathrm{O}_3$  pollution is still found in many regions across China, posing the more complex challenge of simultaneous continuous mitigation of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  pollution with an emerging  $\\mathrm{O}_3$  issue. As  $\\mathrm{O}_3$  pollution has been steadily increasing in most parts of eastern China [47-50],  $\\mathrm{O}_3$  issues have recently gained more attention among both the public and academia in China. As illustrated in Fig. 6(b), the average 90th percentiles in daily maximum  $8\\mathrm{h}$ $\\mathrm{O}_3$  concentrations increased by  $20.1\\%$  and  $24.5\\%$  in 74 key cities and BTH, respectively, from 2013 to 2017. Furthermore, densely populated regions of eastern China were exposed to high  $\\mathrm{O}_3$  pollution, increasing the impacts of  $\\mathrm{O}_3$  on human health. The results of the GBD project [51] estimated that respiratory mortality attributable to long-term  $\\mathrm{O}_3$  exposure in adults is between 274000 and 316000 in China, equal to approximately  $25\\% -29\\%$  of the deaths attributable to  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  exposure [16]. It is necessary to integrate  $\\mathrm{O}_3$  and  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  controls to ensure a pollution-free and healthy living environment in the development of ecological civilization in China.", "The biggest challenge of  $\\mathrm{O_3}$  pollution control is rooted in the complexity of atmospheric photochemistry formation, which depends on  $\\mathrm{O_3 - NO_x - VOC}$  sensitivity diagnosis and meteorological conditions [52,53]. A control strategy designed to reduce emissions of  $\\mathrm{NO}_x$  or VOCs for  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  control may result in an unexpected disadvantage for  $\\mathrm{O_3}$  reduction. Strong VOC-limiting systems are found in most urban areas of China [54,55], and  $\\mathrm{O_3}$  in urban areas can be enhanced if few or no VOCs are controlled simultaneously with  $\\mathrm{NO}_x$  [56]. For example, during the Action Plan from 2013 to 2017, anthropogenic emissions of  $\\mathrm{NO}_x$  were reduced by  $21\\%$  but VOCs increased by  $2\\%$  nationwide [57]. Such combinational changes in  $\\mathrm{NO}_x$ -VOC emissions might be beneficial for reducing  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  but not for  $\\mathrm{O_3}$ , as shown by the decreased  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations and increased  $\\mathrm{O_3}$  concentrations during the same period (Fig. 6). It is notable that the increase in  $\\mathrm{O_3}$  concentration during the Action Plan period is also attributable to variation in meteorological conditions—specifically, changing temperature and short-wave radiation. Therefore, full consideration of meteorology and optimal mitigation of precursor emissions are critical in designing an effective  $\\mathrm{O_3}$  control strategy."], "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Results & Findings", "sub_roles": ["Present Core Findings", "Present Quantitative Results"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Quantitative Charts", "sub_types": ["Line Chart"]}, "key_entities": ["PM2.5", "O3", "BTH region", "China National Environmental Monitoring Center", "90th percentiles"], "concise_summary": "The figure shows trends in PM2.5 and O3 concentrations between 2013 and 2017 in BTH and 74 key cities, highlighting a decrease in PM2.5 and an increase in O3 levels.", "inferred_insight": "Despite reductions in PM2.5, O3 pollution increased, posing challenges in simultaneous mitigation and highlighting the need for integrated pollution control strategies in China."}

content.title: Figure 7. Emission ratios of  SO_2  to  CO_{2} , and CO to  CO_{2}  by Asian regions. CHN is China, OEA is Other East Asia, IND is India, OSA is Other South Asia, SEA is Southeast Asia, CA is Central Asia, and RA is Russia Asia.
content.summary: The figure shows emission ratios of SO2 to CO2 and CO to CO2 across Asian regions, highlighting variations in emission characteristics.
content.insight: The ratios suggest combustion efficiency and emission control levels, with higher SO2/CO2 indicating coal use and low controls, while CO/CO2 shows combustion efficiency discrepancies, particularly low in India's and Southeast Asia's residential sectors.

content.title: Fig. 3. The emission factor of the total PM, and for its chemical constituents as a function of fuel sulphur content (mass-based percentage), based on the data from the second IMO GHG study (Buhaug et al., 2009). Lines indicate the PM component emission factors in STEAM2. The emission factors of the total PM,  SO_4  and  H_2O  are linearly dependent on the fuel sulphur content. The scale used for total PM,  SO_4  and associated  H_2O  is different (left-hand axis) from the scale used for EC, OC and ash (right-hand axis).
content.summary: The figure illustrates the relationship between fuel sulphur content and emission factors of total PM and its constituents, using data from the STEAM2 model and Buhaug study.
content.insight: The linear dependence of total PM, SO4, and associated H2O emission factors on sulphur content implies reductions in sulphur content could significantly decrease these emissions, despite constant emissions from EC, OC, and ash.

metadata.full_json: {"id": "img_104", "source_md": "Extension of an assessment model of ship traffic e.md", "save_path": "C:\\liuxingyu\\multisubspace-data\\predataprocess\\paper_md\\Extension of an assessment model of ship traffic e\\figure_3.jpg", "title": "Fig. 3. The scale used for total PM,  SO_4  and associated  H_2O  is different (left-hand axis) from the scale used for EC, OC and ash (right-hand axis).", "references": ["The sulphur content of the fuel has a crucial influence on the PM emissions. The dependency of PM emission factor on fuel sulphur content was modelled according to Buhaug et al. (2009), as presented in Fig. 3. The emission factors of the total PM,  $\\mathrm{SO}_4$  and associated  $\\mathrm{H}_2\\mathrm{O}$  (i.e.  $\\mathrm{H}_2\\mathrm{O}$  attached to sulphate) are assumed to be linearly dependent on the fuel sulphur content, whereas the emission factors of EC, OC and ash are independent of this factor in STEAM2. The emissions of PM could therefore not be eradicated totally, even if sulphur would be completely eliminated from ship fuels (Winnes and Fridell, 2010b; Buhaug et al., 2009). The measured total mass of particulate matter as defined here includes also the associated  $\\mathrm{H}_2\\mathrm{O}$ ; the amount of which may substantially vary according to the experimental set-up and conditions during the exhaust measurements."], "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Results & Findings", "sub_roles": ["Present Core Findings", "Compare Experimental Performance"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Quantitative Charts", "sub_types": ["Line Chart", "Scatter Plot"]}, "key_entities": ["Emission Factor", "Total PM", "SO4", "H2O", "Fuel Sulphur Content", "STEAM2", "Buhaug"], "concise_summary": "The figure illustrates the relationship between fuel sulphur content and emission factors of total PM and its constituents, using data from the STEAM2 model and Buhaug study.", "inferred_insight": "The linear dependence of total PM, SO4, and associated H2O emission factors on sulphur content implies reductions in sulphur content could significantly decrease these emissions, despite constant emissions from EC, OC, and ash."}

content.title: Figure 1. (a) Source-specific  CO_{2}  emission in each province. Each group of bars represent a region (from left to right): northwest (Xinjiang-Shaanxi), northeast (Neimenggu-Liaoning), north (Beijing-Henan), central (Hubei-Jiangxi), southwest (Xizang-Guangxi), and southeast (Jiangsu-Taiwan). Ten provinces and municipalities with emissions lower than  1000Ggyr^{-1}  were shown in detail in (b): Gansu (GS), Ningxia (NX), Qinghai (QH), Shaanxi (SX), Beijing (BJ), Tianjin (TJ), Chongqing (CQ), Shanghai (SH), Hainan (HN), and Taiwan (TW). Macao and Hong Kong have minimal emissions:  0.5Gg  in Macao, consisting of  0.4Gg  from forest fires and  0.1Gg  from grassland fires; and  24.7Gg  in Hong Kong, consisting of  20.6Gg  ( 83\% ) from forest fires,  2.7Gg  ( 11\% ) from grassland fires,  0.7Gg  from shrubland, and  0.7Gg  from cropland fires.
content.summary: The figure shows CO2 emissions by source in Chinese provinces, with notable contributions from forests, grasslands, cropland, and shrubland fires. It highlights provinces with lower emissions in detail.
content.insight: The figure emphasizes differential regional contributions to CO2 emissions, with forest fires being the largest contributor nationwide, varying regionally with agricultural and crop residue burning.

content.title: Figure 1. (a) Collection of regional emission inventories (US-EPA, Environ Canada, TNO-EMEP, MIX (MICS-Asia III), EDGARv4.3 for the global air pollutants and their use for world countries in data set HTAP v2.2 (b) Regional relative contribution to 2010 pollutant emissions (upper panel). Asian emissions have been divided into China, India and other Asia fractions from the MIX database. The region "rest of the world" has been disaggregated into Oceania, Africa, Middle East, Central/South America and other countries making use of the EDGAR v4.3 inventory. Global sector-specific anthropogenic emissions of gaseous pollutants and particulate matter components for the year 2010 (lower panel). Global absolute emissions are reported on top of each bar in Tg species per year. Large scale open-biomass burning is not included in the analysis. (c) Temporal profiles with relative factors varying around 1/12 and applied on the yearly emissions of the different data sources (US-EPA for US and Canada, EMEP-TNO for Europe with compound-specific variation of the agricultural temporal profiles, EDGAR temporal profiles for the Northern Hemisphere and MICS profiles for Asia).

content.title: Fig. 3. The distributions of estimated  SO_2  emissions from coal-fired power plants in Guizhou in 2005, using Monte Carlo simulation. The black and red bars represent the results from unit-based and sector-based methods, respectively.
content.summary: Comparison of SO2 emission distributions from coal-fired power plants in Guizhou in 2005 using unit-based and sector-based methods via Monte Carlo simulation.
content.insight: Monte Carlo simulation confirms that the sector-based method produces significantly greater uncertainty in SO2 emission estimates than the unit-based method, emphasizing the advantages of detailed source-level data.

The black and red bars represent the results from unit-based and sector-based methods, respectively.", "references": ["In this study, such a test is conducted for one province in China as an example, Guizhou, where coal with extremely high sulfur content is used in power generation. Compared to  $\\mathrm{NO}_x$ , the  $95\\%$  CI of  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$  emission is relatively large, reaching  $-40\\% \\sim 42\\%$ , and the variation of sulfur contents is estimated to contribute almost  $90\\%$  to that uncertainty. Employing a unit-based method, the  $95\\%$  CIs for all species are significantly reduced, with the largest ranging  $-9\\% \\sim 18\\%$ , for  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and the smallest  $-8\\% \\sim 8\\%$ , for  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$ . Figure 3 shows the difference in distributions of  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$  emissions applying the two methods. The test confirms that the uncertainties of provincial and national emissions from power plants may be larger using a sector-based method compared to a unit-based one. Lacking detailed source information, however, such judgment cannot be easily extrapolated to other sectors without similar tests.", "Abstract. The uncertainties of a national, bottom-up inventory of Chinese emissions of anthropogenic  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\\mathrm{NO}_{\\mathrm{x}}$ , and particulate matter (PM) of different size classes and carbonaceous species are comprehensively quantified, for the first time, using Monte Carlo simulation. The inventory is structured by seven dominant sectors: coal-fired electric power, cement, iron and steel, other industry (boiler combustion), other industry (non-combustion processes), transportation, and residential. For each parameter related to emission factors or activity-level calculations, the uncertainties, represented as probability distributions, are either statistically fitted using results of domestic field tests or, when these are lacking, estimated based on foreign or other domestic data. The uncertainties (i.e.,  $95\\%$  confidence intervals around the central estimates) of Chinese emissions of  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\\mathrm{NO}_{\\mathrm{x}}$ , total PM,  $\\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ ,  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , black carbon (BC), and organic carbon (OC) in 2005 are estimated to be  $-14\\% \\sim 13\\%$ ,  $-13\\% \\sim 37\\%$ ,  $-11\\% \\sim 38\\%$ ,  $-14\\% \\sim 45\\%$ ,  $-17\\% \\sim 54\\%$ ,  $-25\\% \\sim 136\\%$ , and  $-40\\% \\sim 121\\%$ , respectively. Variations at activity levels (e.g., energy consumption or industrial production) are not the main source of emission uncertainties. Due to narrow classification of source types, large sample sizes, and relatively high data quality, the coal-fired power sector is estimated to have the smallest emission uncertainties for all species except BC and OC. Due to poorer source classifications and a wider range of estimated emission factors, considerable uncertainties of  $\\mathrm{NO}_{\\mathrm{x}}$  and PM emissions from cement production and boiler combustion in other industries are found. The probability distributions of emission factors for biomass burning, the largest source of BC and OC, are fitted based on very limited domestic field measurements, and special caution should thus be taken interpreting", "Uncertainties of emission factors for coal-fired power plants have been analyzed in our previous study combining bootstrap and Monte Carlo simulation with consideration of boiler type, fuel quality, and emission control device (Zhao et al., 2010). Those results are used in this work. The release ratios of sulfur and ash are estimated to be  $90\\%$  and  $69\\%$ , respectively, both with beta distributions. Regarding the sulfur and ash contents of coal, the probability"], "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Results & Findings", "sub_roles": ["Compare Experimental Performance", "Present Quantitative Results"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Quantitative Charts", "sub_types": ["Histogram"]}, "key_entities": ["SO2 emissions", "coal-fired power plants", "Guizhou", "Monte Carlo simulation", "unit-based method", "sector-based method", "probability distributions"], "concise_summary": "Comparison of SO2 emission distributions from coal-fired power plants in Guizhou in 2005 using unit-based and sector-based methods via Monte Carlo simulation.", "inferred_insight": "Monte Carlo simulation confirms that the sector-based method produces significantly greater uncertainty in SO2 emission estimates than the unit-based method, emphasizing the advantages of detailed source-level data."}

This large discrepancy may come mainly from the difference between the updated domestic emission factors used in this study, and the foreign emission factor database that Ohara et al. The estimated uncertainty ranges for  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$  and  $\\mathrm{NO}_{\\mathrm{x}}$  emissions are very similar among the available studies, while those of PM fractions of different sizes or carbonaceous species are significantly reduced. For example, the  $95\\%$  CIs of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , BC, and OC emissions are estimated to be  $-17\\sim 54\\%$ ,  $-25\\% \\sim 136\\%$ , and  $-40\\% \\sim 121\\%$  in this study, respectively, much lower than  $-57\\% \\sim 130\\%$ ,  $-68\\% \\sim 208\\%$ , and  $-72\\% \\sim 258\\%$  recently derived by Zhang et al. (2009d). The uncertainties of a national, bottom-up inventory of Chinese emissions of anthropogenic  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\\mathrm{NO}_{\\mathrm{x}}$ , and particulate matter (PM) of different size classes and carbonaceous species are comprehensively quantified, for the first time, using Monte Carlo simulation. The inventory is structured by seven dominant sectors: coal-fired electric power, cement, iron and steel, other industry (boiler combustion), other industry (non-combustion processes), transportation, and residential. For each parameter related to emission factors or activity-level calculations, the uncertainties, represented as probability distributions, are either statistically fitted using results of domestic field tests or, when these are lacking, estimated based on foreign or other domestic data. The uncertainties (i.e.,  $95\\%$  confidence intervals around the central estimates) of Chinese emissions of  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\\mathrm{NO}_{\\mathrm{x}}$ , total PM,  $\\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ ,  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , black carbon (BC), and organic carbon (OC) in 2005 are estimated to be  $-14\\% \\sim 13\\%$ ,  $-13\\% \\sim 37\\%$ ,  $-11\\% \\sim 38\\%$ ,  $-14\\% \\sim 45\\%$ ,  $-17\\% \\sim 54\\%$ ,  $-25\\% \\sim 136\\%$ , and  $-40\\% \\sim 121\\%$ , respectively. Variations at activity levels (e.g., energy consumption or industrial production) are not the main source of emission uncertainties. Due to narrow classification of source types, large sample sizes, and relatively high data quality, the coal-fired power sector is estimated to have the smallest emission uncertainties for all species except BC and OC. Due to poorer source classifications and a wider range of estimated emission factors, considerable uncertainties of  $\\mathrm{NO}_{\\mathrm{x}}$  and PM emissions from cement production and boiler combustion in other industries are found. The probability distributions of emission factors for biomass burning, the largest source of BC and OC, are fitted based on very limited domestic field measurements, and special caution should thus be taken interpreting", "Uncertainties introduced by energy statistics and applications of non-Chinese emission factors have been estimated in previous Chinese emission inventory studies. Streets et al. (2003) applied expert judgment, in which the coefficients of variation (i.e., standard deviation divided by the mean) of the activity levels were assumed based on measures of economic development and perceived statistical quality, and those of emission factors were based on the reliability ratings of U.S. emission factors (USEPA, 2002). They estimated that the uncertainties of Chinese emissions varied from  $-12\\% \\sim 13\\%$  for  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$  to  $-83\\% \\sim 495\\%$  for organic carbon (expressed as the lower and upper bounds of a  $95\\%$  confidence interval, CI, around a central estimate). To investigate discrepancies in the trends of bottom-up  $\\mathrm{NO}_{\\mathrm{x}}$  emissions and of the  $\\mathrm{NO}_2$  column by satellite, Zhang et al. (2007a) set alternative emission scenarios in which energy statistics from different sources and emission factors with different control levels were applied. They concluded that  $\\mathrm{NO}_{\\mathrm{x}}$  emissions in east-central China in 2004 could range from 7.9 to  $9.3\\mathrm{Tg}$ , and the annual growth rate of emissions from 1995 to 2004 could range from  $5.5\\%$  to  $7.1\\%$ . Other studies conducted preliminary uncertainty analysis of certain species or sectors in China using statistical methods. Bond et al. (2004) assumed that most emission factors of particulate matter (PM) followed lognormal distributions (with the exception of those of diesel vehicles, which had a gamma distribution) and evaluated the uncertainties of global black carbon (BC) and organic carbon (OC) emissions. The  $95\\%$  CIs for Chinese emissions were estimated to be  $-37\\% \\sim 147\\%$  and  $-44\\% \\sim 104\\%$ , respectively. Wu et al. (2010) focused on", "In this study, therefore, the uncertainties (expressed as the  $95\\%$  CI around the central estimate) of a bottom-up emission inventory in China is evaluated with Monte Carlo simulations, combining comprehensive field measurements of domestic emission factors and investigations of activity levels. The species include  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\\mathrm{NO}_{\\mathrm{x}}$ , total PM,  $\\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ ,  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , BC, and OC. The target year is 2005 because it has the most available data. Determining the probability distributions of all of the related parameters is the main undertaking of this study. Section 2 reviews bottom-up emission inventory methods and analyzes the uncertainties of emission source fractions and corresponding activity levels. Section 3 is a thorough analysis of emission factors by sector and fuel type. Section 4 presents the results and related discussion of emission uncertainties by sector, sensitivity analysis, reliability and limitations of the analysis, and comparisons with other studies. Section 5 summarizes the present study.", "Chinese emissions in 2005 with  $95\\%$  CIs are shown in Table 1 by sector. To be consistent with previous studies, emissions from industrial boilers, cement plants, iron and steel plants, and other industrial processes are aggregated into a single category of industry emissions (see Table S8 in the Supplement for emissions and uncertainties in these sub-sectors). The uncertainties (expressed as the  $95\\%$  CI around the central value) are estimated to be  $-14\\% \\sim 13\\%$ ,  $-13\\% \\sim 37\\%$ ,  $-11\\% \\sim 38\\%$ ,  $-14\\% \\sim 45\\%$ ,  $-17\\% \\sim 54\\%$ ,  $-25\\% \\sim 136\\%$ , and  $-40\\% \\sim 121\\%$  for total emissions of  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\\mathrm{NO}_{\\mathrm{x}}$ , PM,  $\\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ ,  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , BC, and OC respectively (see Fig. S2 for the output distributions of those species by Monte Carlo simulation).", "the result follow. The PM emission characteristics of residential coal combustion in China were tested by different researchers (Zhang and Smith, 2000; Chen et al., 2005; Zhang et al., 2008b), yielding varied emission factors. Very few estimates of the emission factors of Chinese combustion of biomass can be found, however, except for a series of measurements by Li et al. (2007a, b, 2009b). Those measurements were made in four provinces with different types of biomass. As no other domestic tests are available for direct comparisons, systematic biases in these available measurements cannot be ruled out. In this study, we adopt the results from Li et al. (2007a, b, 2009b) fully, lacking further domestic evidence. Given potential high uncertainties, however, a comparison test is conducted for OC emitted by its leading source, biomass combustion, in which the uncertainties of emission factors obtained from Li et al. (2007a, b, 2009b) are replaced with those of foreign studies (Andreae and Merlet, 2001; Streets et al., 2001; Bond et al., 2004). In this case the  $95\\%$  CI of OC emission from biomass combustion changes from  $-58\\sim 216\\%$  to  $-31\\%\\sim 311\\%$ , suggesting caution in interpretation of results based on limited domestic field measurements (see also the discussion in Sect. 4.3).", "The situations for emission factors with higher reliability (i.e., A and B) are more complicated. For coal-fired power plants, the data on which the probability distributions for most parameters are fitted are obtained from both thorough field measurements by the authors and published studies by others (see details in Zhao et al., 2010). These support different emission factors across burner types, fuel qualities, and emission control levels. The uncertainty analysis for this sector are thus the most reliable in this study. Such confidence, however, does not apply to the residential sector, particularly biomass burning, even though the emission factors meet A or B criteria. As discussed in Sect. 4.1, the data on which the distributions are based are limited in number and systematic bias may result in this sector. In other words, the reliability of uncertainty analysis of biomass burning is in fact poor despite the statistical strength of data fitting. Given its high contributions to uncertainties in emission estimates, more field measurements of emissions from biomass burning in China are urgently needed.", "Fig. 4. The difference may result in part from omission in bottom-up inventories of anthropogenic  $\\mathrm{NO}_{\\mathrm{x}}$  sources of entirely different character, such as microbial decomposition of organic wastes associated with the human-animal food chain and applications of chemical fertilizer (Wang et al., 2004). Moreover, emissions vary considerably across regions of China (Wang et al., 2007; Zhao and Wang, 2009) and seasons (Zhang et al., 2007a), and emission uncertainties differentiated accordingly may be larger than national estimates. In order to compare better emission estimates derived from satellite, aircraft, or surface observations, therefore, further investigation of the uncertainties of bottom-up inventories by regions and seasons is needed. content.title: Fig. 2. Trends in national average precipitation pH and total  SO_2  emissions between 1995 and 2017. Precipitation pH data were obtained from 74 sites with continuous observations in the Acid Rain Monitoring Networks [10]; the blue belt indicates the interval within one standard deviation of the mean. Emission data for  SO_2  were derived from the MEIC model.
content.summary: The figure illustrates trends in national average precipitation pH and total SO2 emissions in China from 1995 to 2017, showing a correlation between pH values and emissions.
content.insight: There is a strong negative correlation between SO2 emissions and precipitation pH, with significant policy impact noted during the 11th Five-Year Plan, highlighting past and ongoing efforts to control pollution.

The trough of average precipitation pH values and the peak of total  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$  emissions both took place in 2006, when about one third of China's total territory area was at risk of acid rain. Consequently, the total  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$  emissions increased by nearly  $50\\%$  during 2002-2006, driven by a surge in coal consumption (Fig. 2), which worsened acid rain pollution nationwide [10]. This situation was improved during the 11th Five-Year Plan (FYP) (2006-2010), which set a concrete target to mitigate total  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$  emissions by  $10\\%$ . content.title: Fig. 10. Relative difference between the SOA concentrations averaged in July with and without biogenic emissions ug/m3
content.summary: Map showing relative difference in SOA concentrations in July with and without biogenic emissions over China.
content.insight: Biogenic emissions are significant contributors to SOA during summer in China, impacting air quality, particularly in urban areas where controlling anthropogenic emissions could help reduce SOA levels.

metadata.full_json: {"id": "img_89", "source_md": "Estimation of biogenic VOC emissions and their cor.md", "save_path": "C:\\liuxingyu\\multisubspace-data\\predataprocess\\paper_md\\Estimation of biogenic VOC emissions and their cor\\figure_10.jpg", "title": "Fig. 10. Relative difference between the SOA concentrations averaged in July with and without biogenic emissions ug/m3", "references": ["As shown in Fig. 10, biogenic emissions are the most important contributors to SOA in summer. In China, biogenic emissions account for approximately  $70\\%$  of SOA. In most areas, biogenic emissions are the most crucial contributors; even in areas where there are no significant isoprene emissions, the relative contribution of biogenic", "Biogenic volatile organic compounds (BVOC) play an important role in global environmental chemistry and climate. In the present work, biogenic emissions from China in 2017 were estimated based on the Model of Emissions of Gases and Aerosols from Nature (MEGAN). The effects of BVOC emissions on ozone and secondary organic aerosol (SOA) formation were investigated using the WRF-CMAQ modeling system. Three parallel scenarios were developed to assess the impact of BVOC emissions on China's ozone and SOA formation in July 2017. Biogenic emissions were estimated at  $23.54\\mathrm{Tg / yr}$ , with a peak in the summer and decreasing from southern to northern China. The high BVOC emissions across eastern and southwestern China increased the surface ozone levels, particularly in the BTH (Beijing-Tianjin-Hebei), SCB (Sichuan Basin), YRD (Yangtze River Delta) and central PRD (Pearl River Delta) regions, with increases of up to  $47\\mu \\mathrm{g}\\mathrm{m}^{-3}$  due to the sensitivity of VOC-limited urban areas. In summer, most SOA concentrations formed over China are from biogenic sources (national average of  $70\\%$ ). And SOA concentrations in YRD and SCB regions are generally higher than other regions. Excluding anthropogenic emissions while keeping biogenic emissions unchanged results that SOA concentrations reduce by  $60\\%$  over China, which indicates that anthropogenic emissions can interact with biogenic emissions then facilitate biogenic SOA formation. It is suggested that controlling anthropogenic emissions would result in reduction of both anthropogenic and biogenic SOA.", "As shown in Fig. 7d-e, ozone concentration simulated by NB case is significantly higher than NA case which indicated that effect of biogenic emissions on ozone concentration is less than that of anthropogenic emissions. To clarify the change in ozone concentration after removal of BVOC, Fig. 8 shows the relative difference in ozone concentration with/without BVOC emissions. The high BVOC emissions across the eastern and southwestern areas of China increased  $\\mathrm{DM1O_3}$", "Fig. 9 presents the SOA concentrations simulated by base case, NA and NB case, respectively. In summer, SOA concentrations in central and eastern China typically exceed  $1\\mu \\mathrm{g}\\mathrm{m}^{-3}$  and can reach up to  $3\\mu \\mathrm{g}\\mathrm{m}^{-3}$ . There is also a relatively high SOA concentration in other southern provinces and the Sichuan Basin. The simulated SOA concentrations are low in northern and northeastern China. Removing anthropogenic VOC emissions in July causes a decrease of SOA in major areas by relative change of approximately  $60\\%$  while eliminating biogenic emissions results in an approximately  $70\\%$  reduction of the simulated SOA concentrations. Therefore, the removal of biogenic emissions affects both biogenic and anthropogenic compounds of SOA.", "Fig. 8. {"id": "img_204", "source_md": "Source apportionment of volatile organic compounds.md", "save_path": "C:\liuxingyu\multisubspace-data\predataprocess\paper_md\Source apportionment of volatile organic compounds\figure_7.jpg", "title": "Fig. 7. Percentages  \%  of different VOC sources to the total VOC mixing ratio (MR), reactivity  (L_{OH)} , ozone formation potential (OFP), and secondary organic aerosol potential (SOAP) under different  PM_{2.5}  concentration ranges.", "references": ["To better understand the differences in levels and reactivity of VOC sources under different air quality, the  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  levels were classified into several groups and the source contributions, OH loss rate, OFP, and SOAP of VOCs were summarized in Fig. 7. Generally, with the increase of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  levels from clean to heavy pollution, the VOC levels also increased from  $40.8 \pm 17.6$  ppbv to  $89.6 \pm 26.3$  ppbv. The same phenomenon was also reported elsewhere (Han et al., 2017; Liu et al., 2017; Hui et al., 2019). Fig. 7 clearly shows that the high  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  pollution was characterized by an increase of industrial sources, which accounted for up to  $27.3\%$ ,  $56.2\%$ ,  $48.4\%$ , and  $57.0\%$  of VOCs mixing ratio, OH loss rate, OFP, and SOAP, respectively. The results here were similar to a previous study that the percentage of industrial sources increased significantly with the increase in VOC levels and this source dominated the VOC origins during an entire severe pollution episode (Hui et al.,", "Volatile organic compounds (VOCs) are important contributors to atmospheric chemical reactivity rate  $(\mathrm{L_{OH}})$ , ozone formation potential (OFP), and secondary organic aerosol formation potential (SOAP). The purpose of this study was to determine the contributions of different VOC sources to  $\mathrm{L_{OH}}$ , OFP, and SOAP using positive matrix factorization (PMF) model. Hourly observation of ambient VOCs at a megacity of Central China was conducted for one month in winter. The  $\mathrm{L_{OH}}$ , OFP, and SOAP of VOC species were firstly estimated. The PMF model was then used to apportion their sources. Five VOC sources including solvent usage, gasoline evaporation, combustion sources, vehicle emissions, and industrial sources were apportioned with their average contributions of  $11.6\%$ ,  $14.2\%$ ,  $39.0\%$ ,  $22.5\%$ , and  $12.6\%$ , respectively. Industrial sources were the highest contributors to the  $\mathrm{L_{OH}}$  and SOAP with average contributions of  $32.2\%$  and  $32.8\%$ , respectively, while vehicle emissions contributed most to the OFP  $(27.8\%)$ . This study highlighted that the VOCs and ozone pollution control should give priority to the reactivity (OFP and SOAP) based strategies rather than concentration contribution-based strategies.", "Fig. 6. Percentages  $(\%)$  of different VOC sources to the total VOC mixing ratio (MR), OH loss rate  $\mathrm{(L_{OH})}$ , ozone formation potential (OFP), and secondary organic aerosol potential (SOAP).", "The limitations of this study were from several aspects: one is the limited known chemistry properties of VOC species; the other is the underestimation of the current modelling; and the third is the assumption of the PMF model. For instance, the ozone reactivity scales for 118 VOC species under different  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  conditions were summarized by Carter (2013). Similarly, Atkinson and Arey (2003) summarized the atmospheric degradation of about 190 VOC species (known as the rate constant  $k_{OH}$ ). A list of the SOAPs relative to toluene for 113 VOC species was also reported by Derwent et al. (2010). Despite the great efforts have been made to study the photochemical characteristics of the VOC species, some VOC species' properties are still unknown. They may be important to the atmospheric reactivity, ozone, and secondary aerosol formation potentials. Limited species (i.e., 33 species in this study) introduced into the PMF model suggested the underestimation of  $\mathrm{L_{OH}}$ , OFP, and SOAP. For SOA modelling, the SOA yield method using chamber study or simulation using volatility basis sets (VBS) underestimated the SOA mass (Chen et al., 2017). For the estimation of OFP, despite the MIR method considers the chemical mechanisms and impacts of the  $\mathrm{VOC} / \mathrm{NO}_x$  ratio on ozone production, it may also introduce simulation uncertainties (Ran et al., 2009).", "the flaws and uncertainty existed, this study also provided an additional approach to understand the contributions of various VOC sources to the reactivity of OH loss rate, ozone formation potential (OFP), and secondary organic aerosol potential (SOAP) using the PMF model.", "Dumanoglu, Y., Kara, M., Altiok, H., Odabasi, M., Elbir, T., Bayram, A., 2014. content.title: Fig. 2. Concentrations of VOC components during dry and wet seasons from 2014 to 2019.
content.summary: Figure 2 displays the concentrations of VOC components in dry and wet seasons from 2014 to 2019, highlighting the dominance of OVOCs and the seasonal variations in VOC proportions.
content.insight: The figure illustrates the significant seasonal variation in VOC concentrations with OVOCs being the most prevalent, underscoring their role in atmospheric chemistry and the impact of pollution control measures.

metadata.full_json:
{"id": "img_148", "source_md": "Long-term observations of oxygenated volatile orga.md", "save_path": "C:\liuxingyu\multisubspace-data\predataprocess\paper_md\Long-term observations of oxygenated volatile orga\figure_2.jpg", "title": "Fig. 2. Concentrations of VOC components during dry and wet seasons from 2014 to 2019.", "references": ["A total of 17 species of VOCs were monitored in this study by PTR-MS from 2014 to 2019, including 6 species classified as OVOCs (methanol, acetaldehyde, acetone, formic acid, acetic acid, and MEK); 5 aromatic hydrocarbons (benzene, toluene, styrene, C8 and C9 aromatics); 3 classified as biogenic species (isoprene, MVK + MACR, and monoterpenes); and 2 species that, in addition to benzene, were measured as tracers (acetonitrile and DMS). The compounds, the respective  $\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{z}$  of their parent ion signal, and their detection limits are provided in Table S3. As shown in Fig. 2, the average of the sum of the VOC concentrations in the dry season over the six years ranged from 25.3 to 41.0 ppbv, with an overall 6-year average of 33.1 ppbv; for the wet season, the average value ranged from 23.0 to 39.0 ppbv, with an overall 6-year average of 29.4 ppbv. In terms of the proportion of each component, in the six years of dry and wet seasons, OVOCs (70–78%) accounted for the largest proportion, followed by aromatic hydrocarbons (16–26%), whereas the concentrations of biogenic VOCs (BVOCs) and tracers were relatively low (less than 10%). Figure S6 shows the time series of 17 species of VOCs during the sampling period. To evaluate the concentrations of OVOCs in this study, comparative results of the concentrations of six OVOCs in five urban areas in China are listed in Table 1. Compared to Guangzhou and Hong Kong, two other mega-cities in the PRD region, the concentrations of acetaldehyde, acetone, and MEK in Shenzhen were higher than those in Hong", "Oxygenated volatile organic compounds (OVOCs) are important precursors and intermediate products of atmospheric photochemical reactions, which can promote the formation of secondary pollutants such as ozone  $(\mathrm{O}_3)$  and secondary organic aerosol (SOA). However, there have been few studies on the sources of and long-term variation in ambient OVOCs. This study combined sensitive, near real-time measurements of VOCs by proton transfer reaction-mass spectrometry (PTR-MS) with an improved photochemical age parameterization method to quantify daytime sources of OVOCs in an urban atmosphere in China from 2014 to 2019, permitting the observation of the impacts of emission control strategies that were implemented during this period. Temporal variation in six key OVOCs (methanol, acetaldehyde, acetone, methyl ethyl ketone (MEK), formic acid, and acetic acid) were observed. The sum of concentrations of OVOCs was averagely  $13\%$  higher during the dry season (November to April), when winds transported polluted air masses to Shenzhen from the continent, than during the wet season, and peak diurnal levels occurred during the daytime year-round due to photochemical production and higher daytime anthropogenic emissions. The average dry season concentration of OVOCs declined from a peak of 30.3 ppb in 2015 to 18.7 ppb in 2019. The results of source apportionment showed that primary anthropogenic sources contributed the most to methanol, MEK, and acetic acid  $(32 - 51\%)$ ; the dominant sources of acetaldehyde and formic acid were both primary and secondary anthropogenic sources; and biomass burning contributed a small fraction  $(5 - 11\%)$  to the six OVOCs. From 2014 to 2019, contributions from primary anthropogenic sources of OVOCs decreased significantly by  $50 - 60\%$  due to intensive pollution control measures in Shenzhen, whereas pollution control measures had no observable impact on secondary OVOCs, indicating their formation was not limited by availability of their primary VOC precursors.", "As presented in Fig. 1, Shenzhen is located in the PRD of Guangdong Province, China. The sampling site for this study was located on the campus of the Shenzhen Graduate School of Peking University (PKUSZ,  $22.60^{\circ}\mathrm{N}$ ,  $113.97^{\circ}\mathrm{E}$ ) in western Shenzhen, which is predominantly surrounded by green areas, teaching buildings and apartments. No evident anthropogenic emission sources of air pollutants surrounded the sampling site, except a highway approximately  $100\mathrm{m}$  away. Previous studies have shown that the concentrations of air pollutants such as  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and VOCs at this site were approximately equal to their average concentrations in the entire PRD region (Louie et al., 2013; Huang et al., 2018). Therefore, this site was considered suitable for long-term observation of trends in air pollutants in the PRD region. Table S1 summarizes the effective days of VOCs observed in each month from 2014 to 2019, with 1169 days in total over the six years of sampling. During the six-year sampling period, the loss of data was mainly due to instrument failure and bad weather, especially in the wet season due to frequent rainfall, resulting in a higher rate of missing data during", "Shenzhen belongs to the subtropical marine monsoon climate zone. Marked differences in the four seasons were not evident, and the observation time each year was slightly different; therefore, this study divided the entire year into a dry season and a wet season according to the monthly temperature and precipitation. As presented in Figure S1, the period from May to October, with higher temperatures and abundant precipitation, was defined as the wet season, and the period from November to April, with less precipitation, was defined as the dry season. The meteorological data are from the Meteorological Bureau of Shenzhen Municipality (Meteorological Bureau of Shenzhen Municipality, 2019). In addition to the differences in temperature and precipitation, the sources of air masses in Shenzhen during the wet and dry seasons were also very different. Taking January and July 2018 as an example, the 48-h backward trajectories at  $100\mathrm{m}$  above ground level in Shenzhen  $(113.9^{\circ}\mathrm{E}, 22.6^{\circ}\mathrm{N})$  were calculated beginning at 12:00 UTC using the Hybrid Single Particle Lagrangian Integrated Trajectory (HYSPLIT) model (Draxler and Rolph, 2003). The results show that the origin of the air mass passing through Shenzhen in different seasons was very different: during the dry season, the air mass mainly came from the northern mainland, and during the wet season, it mainly came from the South China Sea, which has also been explained in detail in our previous studies (Zou et al., 2017). In addition, referencing existing studies of air pollution in the Pearl River Delta, many scholars have adopted a method of dividing the wet and dry seasons (Cui et al., 2015). Figure S2 shows the changes in major meteorological parameters during the six-year observation period in Shenzhen. Analysis of major meteorological parameters in Shenzhen (Fig S2) did not indicate significant interannual variability in temperature, rainfall, humidity or wind conditions that could have substantially influenced the long-term variation in atmospheric pollutants observed in this study.", "It should be emphasized that because the sampling data of this study span six years, in addition to the above assumptions, one more assumption should be added: (4) during the sampling period, there was no significant change in the ratio of OVOC anthropogenic emissions to tracers (benzene) in Shenzhen. The emission reduction strategy of Shenzhen is to reduce emission sources of pollutants as a whole, such as replacing gasoline vehicles with electric vehicles and closing down or moving VOC-emitting enterprises to other areas. These measures have reduced emissions of VOCs, but the emission ratio among specific VOC species is relatively stable. Toluene/benzene (T/B) was considered a good indicator to judge the contribution of motor vehicle sources and solvent use to VOC emissions (Nelson and Quigley, 1984; Wang et al., 2002; Araizaga et al., 2013). Figure S5 shows the changes in the concentrations of benzene and toluene and T/B from 2014 to 2019. The concentrations of benzene and toluene in the six years showed a downward trend, while T/B remained at approximately 5, which also indicated", "In this study, the six-year average of VOCs in the dry season was approximately  $13\%$  higher than that in the wet season. As shown in Figure S7, the concentrations of OVCs, aromatic hydrocarbons and tracers in the wet season were lower than those in the dry season, while the BVOC concentration was higher in the wet season. As a highly developed urban area, Shenzhen was active in anthropogenic activities year-round, and there was no obvious seasonal difference in anthropogenic emissions. Therefore, it is more likely that seasonal meteorological differences lead to variations in VOC concentrations between wet and dry seasons. For example, the air masses passing through Shenzhen during the dry season were predominantly from northern inland regions, and the concentration of pollutants carried by inland air masses was higher than that of marine air masses (He et al., 2011; Huang et al., 2020). In addition, the dry season had a lower boundary layer and less precipitation, which was also conducive to the accumulation of pollutants. However, the concentration of BVOCs during the wet season was significantly higher than during the dry season, as shown in Figure S7 (g), (h) and (i), primarily because the higher light levels and solar radiation during the wet season led to accelerated release rates of VOCs by plants (Loreto et al., 2010). It should be pointed out that the data collection frequency in the dry season  $(\sim 73\%)$  was much higher than that in the wet season  $(\sim 35\%)$ , which may lead to relatively higher uncertainty of the conclusion for the wet season.", "The average diurnal variations in mixing ratios of  $\mathrm{O_3}$  and selected VOCs during the dry and wet seasons are shown in Fig. 4. To more clearly compare the diurnal variation trends of the pollutants, all concentrations were normalized (divided by their respective average concentrations). The diurnal variations of the same species during the dry and wet seasons were similar, but  $\mathrm{O_3}$  and OVOCs rose relatively more during the daytime during the warmer wet season. The diurnal variation of benzene showed two peaks corresponding to the morning and afternoon traffic emissions, but the concentration decreased in the afternoon, which may", "be due to the dilution caused by the uplift of the planetary boundary layer (PBL). However, the six OVOCs exhibited a significant upward trend during the daytime. These results suggested that photochemical production and/or daytime anthropogenic activities related to OVC emissions were notable and could overwhelm photochemical loss. The diurnal variation trend of acetaldehyde was most similar to that of  $\mathrm{O_3}$ , exhibiting a greater difference in concentration between day and night during the wet season, indicating that the majority of acetaldehyde originated from daytime secondary generation (Chang et al., 2019). The daytime splits of the diurnal curves of methanol, MEK, and acetic acid, similar to benzene, implied that they could have relatively larger contributions from primary emissions sources, such as motor vehicle emissions. (Guo et al., 2011, Zheng et al., 2013). The diurnal variations in VOCs in our previous two individual campaigns, which were carried out during the dry season (Huang et al., 2019, 2020), were almost the same as those in the dry season in this study.", "Fig. 4. Normalized average diurnal variations in  $\mathrm{O_3}$  and typical VOCs during the dry and wet seasons from 2014 to 2019 (shaded part:  $\pm \mathrm{SD}$ ).", "Fig. 5. The average mixing ratios of the measured VOCs and calculated OFPs in the dry and wet seasons from 2014 to 2019.", "The parameterization method based on photochemical aging described in section 2.4 was used to analyze the sources of OVOCs during the daytime (8:00-20:00). Referencing the source analysis results for the OVOCs in Shenzhen from 2014 to 2019 (in Table 2), the measured concentrations of six OVOC species monitored by PTR-MS were in good agreement with the simulated concentrations, and the measured values of each OVOC species were highly correlated with the simulated values (R was between 0.71 and 0.86, except for formic acid, which was 0.62). For the six-year average, the sources of methanol and MEK were similar, with the largest contribution from primary anthropogenic sources  $(47 - 51\%)$  followed by biogenic and background sources. The contributions to acetaldehyde and acetic acid from primary and secondary anthropogenic sources were similar  $(26 - 32\%)$  followed by biogenic sources (approximately  $20\%$ ). In contrast to other OVOCs, acetone was the most affected by regional background concentration  $(37\%)$  which may be because acetone is relatively inactive and widely exists in the atmosphere, and its ground-level background mixing ratios can reach 500 pptv throughout the troposphere under extremely clean conditions (Seinfeld and Pandis, 2016). For formic acid, secondary anthropogenic sources contributed the most  $(34\%)$", "Fig. 6 shows the trends in the relative contributions to OVOCs from 2014 to 2019 during the dry and wet seasons. In general, during the six years from 2014 to 2019, primary anthropogenic sources contributed the most to OVOCs during Shenzhen's dry season, but the proportion exhibited a decreasing trend year over year, and the proportion during the six-year period decreased by approximately  $15\%$  overall. This continuous decreasing trend indicates that the VOC control measures implemented in the northern inland areas, where most of the industry and traffic of Shenzhen is located, had a significant effect on the reduction of primary emissions. The OVOC source compositions during the wet season, when clean marine air masses from the south dominated the sampling site, did not exhibit a clear year-by-year trend. The contributions of secondary anthropogenic sources to OVOC concentrations (except that of methanol) during the wet season were significantly higher (approximately 1.3-1.6 times greater) than those during the dry season, indicating that secondary transformation of NMHCs during the wet season contributed greatly to OVOC concentrations. Biogenic sources reasonably contributed a larger proportion of OVOC concentrations during the wet season, whereas biomass burning contributed a larger fraction of acetaldehyde during the dry season.", "hydrocarbon and 490 tons of NOx. VOC emission reduction in industrial enterprises and the coating industry occurs mainly through upgrading emission standards and closing down industries with high VOC emissions. For example, furniture manufacturing used to be an important industry in Shenzhen but also a key VOC-emitting industry. From 2010 to 2016, 565 coating production lines were removed or relocated in Shenzhen, reducing VOC emissions by more than 30,000 tons every year. Since 2017, Shenzhen has completely banned solvent paints and adhesives in the manufacturing process and replaced them with water-based paints to further reduce VOC emissions, especially the emissions of aldehydes and ketones commonly used in the production of furniture manufacturing (Ecological Environment Bureau of Shenzhen Municipality, 2019). As a commonly used tracer of anthropogenic sources of VOCs, the annual average concentration of benzene during the dry season from 2014 to 2019 was 0.98, 0.81, 0.64, 0.61, 0.49, and 0.45 ppb (Figure S5), respectively, reflecting the decreasing trend in primary VOC emissions in recent years. Therefore, the decrease in OVOC concentrations during the dry season over the six-year study period could primarily be attributed to decreases in the contributions from primary anthropogenic sources. However, the contribution of secondary anthropogenic", "Fig. 7. Change in ambient concentrations of OVOCs contributed by different sources during the dry seasons of 2014-2019.", "PTR-MS was used for continuous online monitoring of atmospheric VOCs in Shenzhen from 2014 to 2019. The results revealed that OVOCs were the most abundant of 17 species of VOCs measured, comprising  $70 - 78\%$ . The six-year mean concentration for the dry season was  $33.1~\mathrm{ppb}$ , approximately  $13\%$  higher than that for the wet season of  $22.9~\mathrm{ppb}$ . There were diurnal variations in OVOC concentrations, with higher concentrations during the daytime. The diurnal variation in acetaldehyde was most similar to that of  $\mathrm{O}_3$ , and it had the largest OFP of the OVOC species, indicating that acetaldehyde was an important intermediate of photochemical reactions. The results from source apportionment based on photochemical aging parameterization revealed that contributions of OVOCs from primary anthropogenic sources exhibited an evident declining trend of  $50 - 60\%$  from 2014 to 2019 during the dry season, indicating that the intensive VOC control measures implemented in the northern inland areas of the sampling site had a significant effect on the reduction of primary emissions. However, to reduce the impact of OVOCs on air quality in Shenzhen, more efforts are needed to control OVOCs from anthropogenic secondary sources by reducing not only their primary VOC precursors but also their complex atmospheric oxidizing capacity, which will finally restrict the production of  $\mathrm{O}_3$  and SOA.", "Understanding primary and secondary sources of ambient carbonyl compounds in Beijing using the PMF model. content.title: Fig. 3. VOC profiles of (a) stack flue gas (SFN and SFM) and (b) fugitive emissions (OLM and BLM). The ID number of each VOCs species is listed in Table S2.
content.summary: Figure 3 shows the VOC profiles of stack flue gas and fugitive emissions, highlighting major components such as ethylene, 1-butene, benzene, acetylene, and styrene in different emission segments.
content.insight: The figure highlights differences in VOC profiles between non-recovery and mechanical coking plants. Understanding these variations helps to identify and control emissions, with potential to improve air quality by replacing non-recovery plants.

metadata.full_json: {"id": "img_195", "source_md": "Segment-based Volatile Organic Compound Emission C.md", "save_path": "C:\\liuxingyu\\multisubspace-data\\predataprocess\\paper_md\\Segment-based Volatile Organic Compound Emission C\\figure_3.jpg", "title": "Fig. 3. VOC profiles of (a) stack flue gas (SFN and SFM) and (b) fugitive emissions (OLM and BLM). The ID number of each VOCs species is listed in Table S2.", "references": ["The source profiles of individual VOC species were also established using the average weight fraction of the four emission segments determined during field sampling, and the results are shown in Fig. 3. As shown in Fig. 3(a), the most abundant components of the stack flue gas in the non-recovery coking plant were ethylene (42.51%), 1-butene (26.27%), benzene (12.11%), acetylene (10.3%), and 2,2-dimethylbutane (2.06%). The sum of C2-C4 alkene hydrocarbons accounted for approximately 69.96% of total VOCs, indicating that the major components released by the non-recovery coking plant were alkene hydrocarbons of low molecular weight (C2-C4). It should be noted that 1-butene and 2,2-dimethylbutane were not appropriate as source indicator species of the stack flue gas in the non-recovery coking plant because of their multiple sources. For example, 1-butene is a major emission species of power plants (Yan et al., 2016), solvent use/print ink (Wang et al., 2014), LPG-related sources (Wang et al., 2014; Zhang et al., 2015), and iron and steel facilities (Tsai et al., 2008), whereas 2,2-dimethylbutane is a potential indicator of vehicular exhaust in many regions of the world (Chang et al., 2006). In contrast to stack flue gas in the non-recovery coking plant, styrene and benzene were the most abundant components of stack flue gas in the mechanical coking plant, accounting for 32.28% and 25.95% of total VOCs, respectively. The next most dominant species with an average weight fraction of 2-15% were ethylene, propane, ethane, m-xylene, toluene, and ethylbenzene. Moreover, high-weight-fraction benzene series were detected in the stack flue gas of the mechanical coking plant. This result was predominantly attributed to the different coking", "Regarding fugitive emissions, the top five species from oven leaks in the mechanical coking plant were benzene, toluene, ethane,  $m$ -xylene, and ethylene, accounting for  $49.3\\%$ ,  $8.43\\%$ ,  $6.64\\%$ ,  $6.60\\%$ , and  $4.06\\%$  of the total VOCs, respectively. These results agree with those of previous studies (Li et al., 2018). As presented in Fig. 3(b), the major species of byproduct plant leaks from the mechanical coking plant were benzene, propane, ethane, ethylene,  $n$ -pentane,  $n$ -butane, isobutene, 1-butene, toluene, and propylene, accounting for  $34.34\\%$ ,  $9.03\\%$ ,  $7.95\\%$ ,  $7.15\\%$ ,  $5.04\\%$ ,  $4.35\\%$ ,  $4.04\\%$ ,  $2.67\\%$ ,  $2.58\\%$ , and  $2.38\\%$  of the total VOCs, respectively. The sum of C2-C4 hydrocarbons accounted for  $32.04\\%$  of the total VOCs, revealing that C2-C4 hydrocarbons were also the main VOC components emitted from leakage from the byproduct production area in the mechanical coking plant. The VOC characteristic of byproduct plant leaks was different from stack flue gas and oven leaks in the mechanical coking plant because the crude material originates from different coking segments. Stack flue gas and oven leaks in a mechanical coking plant have a similar source for coal gasification process and combustion of coke oven gas, whereas byproduct plant leaks in a mechanical coking plant come from the byproduct recovery process of the coal gas.", "The source profiles, emission factors, and chemical reactivity of volatile organic compounds (VOCs) generated by two typical coking plants, one employing a non-recovery process and the other, a mechanical process, in Shanxi Province, a major coking hub in China, were determined for different segments of coke production during normal operations. The primary components in the stack flue gas were ethylene, 1-butene, benzene, acetylene, and 2,2-dimethylbutane for the non-recovery plant and styrene, benzene, and ethylene for the mechanical plant. The fugitive emissions were also monitored at the mechanical plant, and the most abundant species leaking from the oven were benzene, toluene, ethane,  $m$ -xylene, and ethylene, whereas those leaking from byproducts were benzene, propane, ethane, ethylene,  $n$ -pentane,  $n$ -butane, isobutene, 1-butene, toluene, and propylene. The stack flue gas at the non-recovery and mechanical coking plants exhibited VOC emission factors of  $96 \\mathrm{~g} \\mathrm{Mg}^{-1}$ -coke and  $0.4 \\mathrm{~g} \\mathrm{Mg}^{-1}$ -coke, respectively; thus, VOCs released by the former merit greater concern. Since the highest ozone formation potential (OFP) was observed for the stack flue gas at the non-recovery plant ( $80.26 \\mathrm{mg} \\mathrm{m}^{-3}$ ), followed by fugitive oven emissions at the mechanical plant ( $7.22 \\mathrm{mg} \\mathrm{m}^{-3}$ ), controlling these VOCs will significantly reduce their conversion into ozone. Overall, replacing non-recovery coking plants with mechanical coking plants will decrease VOC emissions and improve the ambient air quality in China.", "All monitored VOCs species were categorized into four groups, i.e., alkanes, alkenes, alkynes and aromatics (Table S2). As shown in Fig. 2, aromatics were the most abundant compounds of SFM, OLM, and BLM, accounting for  $69.17\\%$ ,  $72.57\\%$ , and  $45.97\\%$  of the total VOCs, respectively. The VOC patterns which is mainly characterized by aromatics from different emission segments are similar in the same coking plant and that fugitive leakage should not be ignored during the manufacturing process. On the contrary, the proportion of alkenes to total VOCs was  $70.13\\%$  for SFN, implying that the overall source profile varies significantly in different plants with different production engineering.", "As listed in Table S3, COD values between the VOCs profile in SFN and those in previous reported of coking plant (He, 2006; Jia et al., 2009; Shi et al., 2015; U.S. EPA, 2016) analyzed in this study were mostly in the range of 0.5–1, revealing that the profile of SFN differed substantially from the other profiles. However, the VOCs profile of non-recovery coking has either rarely been studied or the few studies have reported very small VOCs emissions from non-recovery coking (U.S. EPA, 2008). The VOCs profile of non-recovery coking plant in this study may be served as a start point for future VOCs emission research in this type coking technology. The COD value was 0.68 between SFN and SFM, which also suggests clear differences in the stack flue gas characteristics of coking plants with different technologies.", "Emission factors are used as essential data for building the emission inventories and for environmental management. Production engineering, equipment, fuel sources, combustion conditions, treatments, etc. are all factors influencing VOCs source emissions. The VOCs EFs for SFN and SFM were  $96\\mathrm{g}\\mathrm{Mg}^{-1}$ -coke and  $0.4\\mathrm{g}\\mathrm{Mg}^{-1}$ -coke, respectively (Fig. 4), indicating that the emission intensity from the stack in a non-recovery coking plant is far greater than that in a mechanical coking plant. As the coke oven gas produced by the coking procedure in a non-recovery coking plant operates under negative pressure, the atmospheric pollutants are predominantly discharged through the stack to the atmosphere. However, the EFs of coke production from the mechanical coking plant only involved estimation of the VOCs emissions from the stack flue gas and neglected fugitive emissions such as coke oven and byproduct plant leaks, which partly explains the small EFs from the stack in the mechanical coking plant. As shown in Fig. 4, the EFs of alkenes and aromatics in SFN and SFM were  $67.15\\mathrm{g}\\mathrm{Mg}^{-1}$  and  $0.29\\mathrm{g}\\mathrm{Mg}^{-1}$ , respectively, representing the groups with highest emission intensity. The major species with high emission intensity in SFN were ethylene, 1-butene, benzene, and acetylene, with EFs of  $40.58\\mathrm{g}\\mathrm{Mg}^{-1}$ ,  $25.26\\mathrm{g}\\mathrm{Mg}^{-1}$ ,  $11.71\\mathrm{g}\\mathrm{Mg}^{-1}$ , and  $9.89\\mathrm{g}\\mathrm{Mg}^{-1}$ , respectively. For SFM, the major species were styrene, benzene, and ethylene, with EFs of  $0.16\\mathrm{g}\\mathrm{Mg}^{-1}$ ,  $0.08\\mathrm{g}\\mathrm{Mg}^{-1}$ , and  $0.04\\mathrm{g}\\mathrm{Mg}^{-1}$ , respectively. The results of this study indicate that phasing out non-recovery coking plants in China and strongly promoting mechanical coking plants instead would help reduce VOCs emissions, especially alkene emissions, which is significant for improving the ambient air quality.", "We measured the VOCs emitted from various segments of coke production at a non-recovery plant and a mechanical coking plant in China and then analyzed the source profiles, emission factors, and chemical reactivity. The VOCs were monitored in the stack flue gas of both the non-recovery (SFN) and the mechanical (SFM) plant as well as in fugitive emissions due to leakage", "from the oven (OLM) and from byproducts (BLM) at the mechanical plant. The primary components in the SFN were ethylene, 1-butene, benzene, acetylene, and 2,2-dimethylbutane, whereas those in the SFM were styrene, benzene, and ethylene. The OLM was dominated by benzene, toluene, ethane,  $m$ -xylene, and ethylene, and the BLM, by benzene, propane, ethane, ethylene,  $n$ -pentane,  $n$ -butane, isobutene, 1-butene, toluene, and propylene. These results imply that acetylene and styrene can serve as indicators of non-recovery and mechanical coking processes, respectively. Furthermore, low-molecular-weight hydrocarbons (C2-C4) are synergetic indicators of byproduct emissions at coking plants. The VOCs emission factors for the SFN and SFM were  $96 \\mathrm{~g} \\mathrm{Mg}^{-1}$ -coke and  $0.4 \\mathrm{~g} \\mathrm{Mg}^{-1}$ -coke, respectively, demonstrating that VOCs in the stack flue gas of non-recovery coking plants should be controlled due to their high emission intensity. By determining the VOC source profile and emission factor for the SFN, this study provides crucial reference data for the existing source profile library and enables us to assess domestic VOC emissions generated by the coking process. The highest source reactivity was exhibited by the SFN ( $80.26 \\mathrm{mg} \\mathrm{m}^{-3}$ ), followed by the OLM ( $7.22 \\mathrm{mg} \\mathrm{m}^{-3}$ ), which suggests that controlling VOCs in the stack flue gas at non-recovery plants and in the fugitive oven emissions at mechanical plants will greatly reduce the conversion of these pollutants into ozone. Finally, replacing non-recovery coking plants with mechanical ones in China will decrease VOC emissions and significantly improve the ambient air quality."], "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Results & Findings", "sub_roles": ["Present Core Findings", "Present Quantitative Results"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Quantitative Charts", "sub_types": ["Bar Chart"]}, "key_entities": ["VOCs", "SFN", "SFM", "OLM", "BLM", "ethylene", "1-butene", "benzene", "acetylene", "styrene"], "concise_summary": "Figure 3 shows the VOC profiles of stack flue gas and fugitive emissions, highlighting major components such as ethylene, 1-butene, benzene, acetylene, and styrene in different emission segments.", "inferred_insight": "The figure highlights differences in VOC profiles between non-recovery and mechanical coking plants. Understanding these variations helps to identify and control emissions, with potential to improve air quality by replacing non-recovery plants."}

content.title: Fig. 6. Percentages  \%  of different VOC sources to the total VOC mixing ratio (MR), OH loss rate  (L_{OH)} , ozone formation potential (OFP), and secondary organic aerosol potential (SOAP).
content.summary: The figure displays the contribution percentages of various VOC sources to metrics such as MR, L_{OH}, OFP, and SOAP.
content.insight: The dominant sources differ across metrics; combustion sources lead in MR, while industrial sources dominate L_{OH} and SOAP, and vehicle emissions are highest for OFP.

{"id": "img_203", "source_md": "Source apportionment of volatile organic compounds.md", "save_path": "C:\liuxingyu\multisubspace-data\predataprocess\paper_md\Source apportionment of volatile organic compounds\figure_6.jpg", "title": "Fig. 6. Percentages  \%  of different VOC sources to the total VOC mixing ratio (MR), OH loss rate  (L_{OH)} , ozone formation potential (OFP), and secondary organic aerosol potential (SOAP).", "references": ["Fig. 6 shows the contributions of different VOC sources to the total VOC mixing ratio. The combustion sources contributed most (39.0% or  $23.9 \pm 13.6$  ppbv) to the total VOCs in winter, followed by vehicle emissions (22.5%,  $14.0 \pm 11.3$  ppbv), gasoline evaporation (14.2%,  $8.83 \pm 7.11$  ppbv), industrial sources (12.6%,  $7.92 \pm 10.2$  ppbv), and solvent usage (11.6%,  $6.98 \pm 5.65$  ppbv), respectively.", "As shown in Fig. 6, industrial sources contributed  $32.2\%$  to the total OH loss rate, followed by solvent usage  $(21.4\%)$ , combustion sources  $(20.4\%)$ , vehicle emissions  $(19.4\%)$ , and gasoline evaporation  $(6.61\%)$  respectively. Usually, vehicle emissions or petrochemical industrial emissions dominate man-made VOC reactivity in urban areas. For instance, motor vehicle emissions (dominated by high fractions of aromatics and alkenes) contributed to  $50\%$  of the calculated VOC reactivity in Pasadena (Borbon et al., 2013). Similarly,  $\mathrm{C}_2 \sim \mathrm{C}_6$  alkenes and aromatics from the petrochemical refinery and motor vehicles accounted for  $42\%$  of the total calculated VOC reactivity  $(7.5~\mathrm{s}^{-1})$  in the Houston/ Galveston Bay region (Gilman et al., 2009). The percentages of vehicle emissions and industrial sources to OH loss rate in this study were lower than those in the two above researches. However, in this study, the VOC reactivity  $(2.79~\mathrm{s}^{-1})$  contributed by vehicle emissions was higher than the traffic-related reactivity  $(1.2~\mathrm{s}^{-1})$  in the Northern Front Range Metropolitan Area of Colorado (Abeleira et al., 2017).", "For the OFP of different sources (Fig. 6), vehicle emissions had the largest contribution  $(27.8\%)$ , followed by industrial sources  $(25.4\%)$ , solvent usage  $(20.9\%)$ , combustion sources  $(18.9\%)$ , and gasoline evaporation  $(7.04\%)$ , respectively. The OFP of vehicle emissions in this study was comparable to a previous study in Wuhan that vehicle exhausts accounted for  $23.4 \pm 1.3\%$  of  $\mathrm{O}_3$  formation (Lyu et al., 2016). However, the contribution of industrial sources to OFP was lower than", "Volatile organic compounds (VOCs) are important contributors to atmospheric chemical reactivity rate  $(\mathrm{L_{OH}})$ , ozone formation potential (OFP), and secondary organic aerosol formation potential (SOAP). The purpose of this study was to determine the contributions of different VOC sources to  $\mathrm{L_{OH}}$ , OFP, and SOAP using positive matrix factorization (PMF) model. Hourly observation of ambient VOCs at a megacity of Central China was conducted for one month in winter. The  $\mathrm{L_{OH}}$ , OFP, and SOAP of VOC species were firstly estimated. The PMF model was then used to apportion their sources. Five VOC sources including solvent usage, gasoline evaporation, combustion sources, vehicle emissions, and industrial sources were apportioned with their average contributions of  $11.6\%$ ,  $14.2\%$ ,  $39.0\%$ ,  $22.5\%$ , and  $12.6\%$ , respectively. Industrial sources were the highest contributors to the  $\mathrm{L_{OH}}$  and SOAP with average contributions of  $32.2\%$  and  $32.8\%$ , respectively, while vehicle emissions contributed most to the OFP  $(27.8\%)$ . This study highlighted that the VOCs and ozone pollution control should give priority to the reactivity (OFP and SOAP) based strategies rather than concentration contribution-based strategies.", "Alkanes  
Fig. 3. Individual contributions  $(\%)$  of different VOC species (outer circular bar plots) and mean contributions  $(\%)$  (inner donut charts) of alkanes, alkenes, acetylene, and aromatics to the OH loss rate (a), ozone formation potential (b), and secondary organic aerosol potential (c).", "Fig. 7. Percentages  $(\%)$  of different VOC sources to the total VOC mixing ratio (MR), reactivity  $\mathrm{(L_{OH})}$ , ozone formation potential (OFP), and secondary organic aerosol potential (SOAP) under different  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentration ranges.", "To better understand the differences in levels and reactivity of VOC sources under different air quality, the  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  levels were classified into several groups and the source contributions, OH loss rate, OFP, and SOAP of VOCs were summarized in Fig. 7. Generally, with the increase of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  levels from clean to heavy pollution, the VOC levels also increased from  $40.8 \pm 17.6$  ppbv to  $89.6 \pm 26.3$  ppbv. The same phenomenon was also reported elsewhere (Han et al., 2017; Liu et al., 2017; Hui et al., 2019). Fig. 7 clearly shows that the high  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  pollution was characterized by an increase of industrial sources, which accounted for up to  $27.3\%$ ,  $56.2\%$ ,  $48.4\%$ , and  $57.0\%$  of VOCs mixing ratio, OH loss rate, OFP, and SOAP, respectively. The results here were similar to a previous study that the percentage of industrial sources increased significantly with the increase in VOC levels and this source dominated the VOC origins during an entire severe pollution episode (Hui et al.,", "2019). On the contrary, the contributions of combustion sources, vehicle emissions, and solvent usage to OH loss rate, OFP, and SOAP decreased with the increase of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  levels. Despite a decreasing trend of combustion sources to the VOC mixing ratio from clean to pollution, it still dominated the VOC origins during the heavy pollution period. Therefore, to alleviate the VOCs pollution, it should prior control combustion sources. From the perspective of VOC reactivity, OFP, and SOAP, reducing VOC emissions from industrial sources would have benefits to both the reduction of VOC levels and SOA during pollution episodes. The results here contributed to better-developing countermeasures of alleviating VOCs, ozone, and particulate matter pollutions.", "The limitations of this study were from several aspects: one is the limited known chemistry properties of VOC species; the other is the underestimation of the current modelling; and the third is the assumption of the PMF model. For instance, the ozone reactivity scales for 118 VOC species under different  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  conditions were summarized by Carter (2013). Similarly, Atkinson and Arey (2003) summarized the atmospheric degradation of about 190 VOC species (known as the rate constant  $k_{OH}$ ). A list of the SOAPs relative to toluene for 113 VOC species was also reported by Derwent et al. (2010). Despite the great efforts have been made to study the photochemical characteristics of the VOC species, some VOC species' properties are still unknown. They may be important to the atmospheric reactivity, ozone, and secondary aerosol formation potentials. Limited species (i.e., 33 species in this study) introduced into the PMF model suggested the underestimation of  $\mathrm{L_{OH}}$ , OFP, and SOAP. For SOA modelling, the SOA yield method using chamber study or simulation using volatility basis sets (VBS) underestimated the SOA mass (Chen et al., 2017). For the estimation of OFP, despite the MIR method considers the chemical mechanisms and impacts of the  $\mathrm{VOC} / \mathrm{NO}_x$  ratio on ozone production, it may also introduce simulation uncertainties (Ran et al., 2009).", "the flaws and uncertainty existed, this study also provided an additional approach to understand the contributions of various VOC sources to the reactivity of OH loss rate, ozone formation potential (OFP), and secondary organic aerosol potential (SOAP) using the PMF model.", "Dumanoglu, Y., Kara, M., Altiok, H., Odabasi, M., Elbir, T., Bayram, A., 2014. img_203.source_reason: 该条目提到清洁生产等措施的有效性以及协同减缓措施的必要性，正是重要的未来研究方向，应进一步探索。

evaluation.extracted_insight: 图表展示了不同 VOC 来源对总 VOC 混合比率、羟基损失率、臭氧形成潜力和二次有机气溶胶潜力的贡献，重点指出燃烧和工业源的主导作用。
evaluation.reason: 该图揭示了 VOCs 不同化学指标的关键源头，为深入研究它们对空气污染的影响提供了有价值的洞察。
